title: X (social network)

infobox.value: March 21, 2006; 20 years ago (2006-03-21), in San Francisco, California, U.S.

**Current:** [Bastrop, Texas](/wiki/Bastrop,_Texas "Bastrop, Texas")[[2]](#cite_note-2) **Formerly**: [Twitter Building](/wiki/Twitter_Building "Twitter Building") ([San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), California)

* [Odeo](/wiki/Odeo "Odeo") (March–October 2006) * [Obvious Corporation](/wiki/Obvious_Corporation "Obvious Corporation") (2006–2007) * [Twitter, Inc.](/wiki/Twitter,_Inc. "Twitter, Inc.") (2007–2023) * [X Corp.](/wiki/X_Corp. "X Corp.") (2023–present)

* [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey") * [Noah Glass](/wiki/Noah_Glass "Noah Glass") * [Biz Stone](/wiki/Biz_Stone "Biz Stone") * [Evan Williams](/wiki/Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur) "Evan Williams (Internet entrepreneur)")
July 15, 2006; 19 years ago (2006-07-15) (as Twitter)  July 23, 2023; 2 years ago (2023-07-23) (as X)

* [Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") * [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") * [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") * [iPadOS](/wiki/IPadOS "IPadOS") * [Amazon Fire Tablet](/wiki/Fire_OS "Fire OS")

infobox.key: We wanted to capture that in the name — we wanted to capture that feeling: the physical sensation that you're buzzing your friend's pocket. It's like buzzing all over the world. So we did a bunch of name-storming, and we came up with the word "twitch", because the phone kind of vibrates when it moves. But "twitch" is not a good product name because it doesn't bring up the right imagery. So we looked in the dictionary for words around it, and we came across the word "[twitter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/twitter "wikt:twitter")", and it was just perfect. The definition was "a short burst of inconsequential information," and "chirps from birds." And that's exactly what the product was.

infobox.value: [Bastrop, Texas](/wiki/Bastrop,_Texas "Bastrop, Texas")[[2]](#cite_note-2) **Formerly**: [Twitter Building](/wiki/Twitter_Building "Twitter Building") ([San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), California) |

**X**, formerly known as **Twitter**,[[b]](#cite_note-4) is an American [microblogging](/wiki/Microblogging "Microblogging") and [social networking service](/wiki/Social_networking_service "Social networking service"), headquartered in [Bastrop, Texas](/wiki/Bastrop,_Texas "Bastrop, Texas"). It is one of the world's largest [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") platforms and one of the [most-visited websites](/wiki/Most-visited_websites "Most-visited websites").[[3]](#cite_note-cnbc-kolodny-5)[[4]](#cite_note-:5-6) Users can share short [text messages](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging"), images, and videos in short posts (commonly and unofficially known as "[tweets](/wiki/Tweet_(social_media) "Tweet (social media)")") and [like](/wiki/Like_button "Like button") other users' content.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7) The platform also includes [direct messaging](/wiki/Direct_message "Direct message"), video and audio calling, bookmarks, lists, communities, [Grok](/wiki/Grok_(chatbot) "Grok (chatbot)") chatbot integration, job search,[[6]](#cite_note-:7-8) and a social audio feature (X Spaces). Users can vote on context added by approved users using the [Community Notes](/wiki/Community_Notes "Community Notes") feature.

The platform was created in March 2006 as Twitter by [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), [Noah Glass](/wiki/Noah_Glass "Noah Glass"), [Biz Stone](/wiki/Biz_Stone "Biz Stone"), and [Evan Williams](/wiki/Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur) "Evan Williams (Internet entrepreneur)"), and was launched in July of that year; initially named **twttr**, it was renamed Twitter some months later. The platform grew quickly; by 2012 more than 100 million users produced 340 million daily tweets.[[7]](#cite_note-Twitter-2012-9) Twitter, Inc., was based in San Francisco, California, and had more than 25 offices around the world.[[8]](#cite_note-:2-10) A signature characteristic of the service initially was that posts were required to be brief. Posts were initially limited to 140 characters, which was changed to 280 characters in 2017. The limitation was removed for [subscribed](/wiki/Subscription_business_model "Subscription business model") accounts in 2023.[[9]](#cite_note-Twitter_500-11) 10% of users produce over 80% of tweets.[[10]](#cite_note-:3-12)[[11]](#cite_note-:8-13) In 2020, it was estimated that approximately 48 million accounts (15% of all accounts) were run by [Internet bots](/wiki/Internet_bot "Internet bot") rather than humans.[[12]](#cite_note-Rodriguez-14)

The service is owned by the American company [X Corp.](/wiki/X_Corp. "X Corp."), which was established to succeed the prior owner [Twitter, Inc.](/wiki/Twitter,_Inc. "Twitter, Inc.") in March 2023 following the [October 2022 acquisition of Twitter](/wiki/Acquisition_of_Twitter_by_Elon_Musk "Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk") by [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") for US$44 billion. Musk stated that his goal with the acquisition was to promote [free speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech_in_the_United_States "Freedom of speech in the United States") on the platform. [Since his acquisition](/wiki/Twitter_under_Elon_Musk "Twitter under Elon Musk"), the platform has been criticized for enabling the increased spread of [disinformation](/wiki/Disinformation "Disinformation")[[13]](#cite_note-:02-15)[[14]](#cite_note-:12-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17) and [hate speech](/wiki/Hate_speech "Hate speech").[[16]](#cite_note-Hickey-2023-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21) [Linda Yaccarino](/wiki/Linda_Yaccarino "Linda Yaccarino") succeeded Musk as [CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") on June 5, 2023, with Musk remaining as the [chairman](/wiki/Chair_(officer) "Chair (officer)") and the [chief technology officer](/wiki/Chief_technology_officer "Chief technology officer").[[20]](#cite_note-yaccarino-ceo-22)[[21]](#cite_note-Miller-2022-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24) In July 2023, Musk announced that Twitter would be rebranded to "X" and the bird logo would be retired, a process which was completed by May 2024.[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-26) In March 2025, X Corp. was acquired by [xAI](/wiki/XAI_(company) "XAI (company)"), Musk's [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") company. The deal, an all-stock transaction, valued X at $33 billion, with a full valuation of $45 billion when factoring in $12 billion in debt. Meanwhile, xAI itself was valued at $80 billion.[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) In July 2025, Yaccarino stepped down from her role as CEO.[[27]](#cite_note-29) The X site has been accused of becoming increasingly [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing "Right-wing") and catering to [hate groups](/wiki/Hate_group "Hate group").[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)

In February 2026, the platform's owner, xAI, was acquired by [SpaceX](/wiki/SpaceX "SpaceX"); this marked the sixth time where the social media platform has changed hands and the third time under Musk's ownership.[[32]](#cite_note-:13-34)[[33]](#cite_note-:03-35)

## History

Main article: [History of Twitter](/wiki/History_of_Twitter "History of Twitter")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of X (social network)](/wiki/Timeline_of_X_(social_network) "Timeline of X (social network)").

### 2006–2021

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Twttr_sketch-Dorsey-2006.jpg/250px-Twttr_sketch-Dorsey-2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Twttr_sketch-Dorsey-2006.jpg)

A sketch, c. 2006, by [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), envisioning an SMS-based [social network](/wiki/Social_network "Social network")

[Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey") claims to have introduced the idea of an individual using an SMS service to communicate to a small group in 2006.[[34]](#cite_note-36) The original [project code name](/wiki/Project_code_name "Project code name") for the service was *Twttr*, an idea that Evan Williams later ascribed to [Noah Glass](/wiki/Noah_Glass_(Twitter) "Noah Glass (Twitter)"),[[35]](#cite_note-37) inspired by [Flickr](/wiki/Flickr "Flickr") and the five-character length of American SMS [short codes](/wiki/Short_code "Short code"). The decision was also partly due to the fact that the domain twitter.com was already in use, and it was six months after the launch of Twttr that the crew purchased the domain and changed the name of the service to *Twitter*.[[36]](#cite_note-38) Work on the project started in February 2006.[[37]](#cite_note-Carlson-2011-39)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| We wanted to capture that in the name — we wanted to capture that feeling: the physical sensation that you're buzzing your friend's pocket. It's like buzzing all over the world. So we did a bunch of name-storming, and we came up with the word "twitch", because the phone kind of vibrates when it moves. But "twitch" is not a good product name because it doesn't bring up the right imagery. So we looked in the dictionary for words around it, and we came across the word "[twitter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/twitter "wikt:twitter")", and it was just perfect. The definition was "a short burst of inconsequential information," and "chirps from birds." And that's exactly what the product was. | — Jack Dorsey[[38]](#cite_note-40) |

The first Twitter prototype, developed by Dorsey and contractor Florian Weber, was used as an internal service for [Odeo](/wiki/Odeo "Odeo") employees.[[37]](#cite_note-Carlson-2011-39) The full version was introduced publicly on July 15, 2006.[[39]](#cite_note-launch-41) In October 2006, Biz Stone, Evan Williams, Dorsey, and other members of Odeo formed Obvious Corporation and acquired Odeo from the investors and shareholders.[[40]](#cite_note-42) Williams fired Glass, who was silent about his part in Twitter's startup until 2011.[[41]](#cite_note-43) Twitter spun off into its own company in April 2007.[[42]](#cite_note-44) The [tipping point](/wiki/Tipping_point_(sociology) "Tipping point (sociology)") for Twitter's popularity was the 2007 [South by Southwest Interactive](/wiki/South_by_Southwest_Interactive "South by Southwest Interactive") (SXSWi) conference. During the event, [Twitter usage](/wiki/Twitter_usage "Twitter usage") increased from 20,000 tweets per day to 60,000.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

The company experienced rapid initial growth thereafter. In 2009, Twitter won the "Breakout of the Year" [Webby Award](/wiki/Webby_Award "Webby Award").[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47) In February 2010, Twitter users were sending 50 million tweets per day.[[46]](#cite_note-48) By March 2010, the company recorded over 70,000 registered applications.[[47]](#cite_note-49) In June 2010, about 65 million tweets were posted each day, equaling about 750 tweets sent each second, according to Twitter.[[48]](#cite_note-50) As noted on [Compete.com](/wiki/Compete.com "Compete.com"), Twitter moved up to the third-highest-ranking [social networking](/wiki/Social_networking "Social networking") site in January 2009 from its previous rank of twenty-second.[[49]](#cite_note-Kazeniac-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Jack_Dorsey_-_TechCrunch_Real-Time_Stream_Crunchup_-_2009.jpg/250px-Jack_Dorsey_-_TechCrunch_Real-Time_Stream_Crunchup_-_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jack_Dorsey_-_TechCrunch_Real-Time_Stream_Crunchup_-_2009.jpg)

[Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), co-founder and former CEO of Twitter at CrunchUp, a [TechCrunch](/wiki/TechCrunch "TechCrunch") event, July 2009

From September through October 2010, the company began rolling out "New Twitter", an entirely revamped edition of twitter.com. Changes included the ability to see pictures and videos without leaving Twitter itself by clicking on individual tweets which contain links to images and clips from a variety of supported websites, including YouTube and [Flickr](/wiki/Flickr "Flickr"), and a complete overhaul of the interface.[[50]](#cite_note-:4-52) In 2019, Twitter was announced to be the 10th most downloaded mobile app of the decade, from 2010 to 2019.[[50]](#cite_note-:4-52)

On March 21, 2012, Twitter celebrated its sixth birthday by announcing that it had 140 million users, a 40% rise from September 2011, who were sending 340 million tweets per day.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54) On June 5, 2012, a modified logo was unveiled through the company blog, removing the text to showcase the slightly redesigned bird as the sole symbol of Twitter.[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56) On December 18, 2012, Twitter announced it had surpassed 200 million [monthly active users](/wiki/Monthly_active_users "Monthly active users").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In September 2013, the company's data showed that 200 million users sent over 400 million tweets daily, with nearly 60% of tweets sent from mobile devices.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

In April 2014, Twitter underwent a redesign that made the site resemble Facebook somewhat, with a profile picture and biography in a column left to the timeline, and a full-width header image with [parallax scrolling](/wiki/Parallax_scrolling "Parallax scrolling") effect.[[c]](#cite_note-58)[[56]](#cite_note-59) Late in 2015, it became apparent that growth had slowed, according to Fortune,[[57]](#cite_note-60) Business Insider,[[58]](#cite_note-61) Marketing Land[[59]](#cite_note-62) and other news websites including Quartz (in 2016).[[60]](#cite_note-63) In 2019, Twitter released another redesign of its user interface.[[61]](#cite_note-64) By the start of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=X_(social_network)&action=edit), Twitter had more than 330 million monthly active users.[[62]](#cite_note-Twitter-65) Twitter then experienced considerable growth during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") in 2020.[[63]](#cite_note-66) The platform also was increasingly used for [misinformation related to the pandemic](/wiki/Misinformation_related_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Misinformation related to the COVID-19 pandemic").[[64]](#cite_note-67) Twitter started marking tweets which contained misleading information, and adding links to fact-checks.[[65]](#cite_note-Roth-2020-68)

In 2021, Twitter began the research phase of [Bluesky](/wiki/Bluesky_(protocol) "Bluesky (protocol)"), an [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") decentralized social media protocol where users can choose which [algorithmic curation](/wiki/Algorithmic_curation "Algorithmic curation") they want.[[66]](#cite_note-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70) The same year, Twitter also released Twitter Spaces, a social audio feature;[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72) "super follows", a way to subscribe to creators for exclusive content;[[70]](#cite_note-73) and a beta of "ticketed Spaces", which makes access to certain audio rooms paid.[[71]](#cite_note-74) Twitter unveiled a redesign in August 2021, with adjusted colors and a new Chirp font, which improves the left-alignment of most Western languages.

### Since 2022

See also: [Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk](/wiki/Acquisition_of_Twitter_by_Elon_Musk "Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/X_logo_2023_original.svg/250px-X_logo_2023_original.svg.png)](/wiki/File:X_logo_2023_original.svg)

The original version of the X logo

These paragraphs are an excerpt from [Twitter under Elon Musk](/wiki/Twitter_under_Elon_Musk "Twitter under Elon Musk").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Twitter_under_Elon_Musk&action=edit)]

[Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") completed [his acquisition of Twitter](/wiki/Acquisition_of_Twitter_by_Elon_Musk "Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk") in October 2022; Musk acted as [CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") of [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") until June 2023 when he was succeeded by [Linda Yaccarino](/wiki/Linda_Yaccarino "Linda Yaccarino").[[72]](#cite_note-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76) Twitter was rebranded to X on July 23, 2023,[[74]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_rebrand-77) and its domain name changed from twitter.com to x.com on May 17, 2024.[[75]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_url-78) Yaccarino resigned on July 9, 2025.[[76]](#cite_note-79)

Now operating as X, the platform closely resembles its predecessor but includes additional features such as long-form texts,[[77]](#cite_note-80) account monetization options,[[78]](#cite_note-81) audio-video calls,[[79]](#cite_note-82) integration with [xAI](/wiki/XAI_(company) "XAI (company)")'s [Grok](/wiki/Grok_(chatbot) "Grok (chatbot)") chatbot,[[80]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_The_Indian_Express-2023-83) job search,[[81]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_HR_Dive-84) and a [repurposing of the platform's verification system as a subscription premium](/wiki/Twitter_Blue_verification_controversy "Twitter Blue verification controversy").[[82]](#cite_note-85) Several legacy Twitter features were removed from the site after Musk acquired Twitter, including Circles,[[83]](#cite_note-86) [NFT](/wiki/Non-fungible_token "Non-fungible token") profile pictures,[[84]](#cite_note-87) and the experimental [pronouns](/wiki/Preferred_gender_pronoun "Preferred gender pronoun") in profiles feature.[[85]](#cite_note-88) Musk's aims included transforming X into a "digital [town square](/wiki/Town_square "Town square")" and an "everything app" akin to [WeChat](/wiki/WeChat "WeChat").[[86]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_Ortutay-2023-89)

Twitter/X has faced significant controversy under Musk's ownership. Issues such as the release of the [Twitter Files](/wiki/Twitter_Files "Twitter Files"), [suspension of ten journalists' accounts](/wiki/December_2022_Twitter_suspensions "December 2022 Twitter suspensions"), and labeling media outlets as "state-affiliated" and restricting their visibility have sparked criticism.[[87]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_Stempel-90)[[88]](#cite_note-91) Despite Musk stepping down as CEO, the platform continues to struggle with challenges such as viral [misinformation](/wiki/Misinformation "Misinformation"),[[89]](#cite_note-92) [hate speech](/wiki/Hate_speech "Hate speech") (especially [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism")), and [child pornography](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography").[[90]](#cite_note-93)[[91]](#cite_note-94)[[92]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_:12-95) In response to allegations it deemed unfair, X Corp. has pursued legal action against nonprofit organizations [Media Matters](/wiki/Media_Matters_for_America "Media Matters for America") and the [Center for Countering Digital Hate](/wiki/Center_for_Countering_Digital_Hate "Center for Countering Digital Hate").[[87]](#cite_note-Twitter_under_Elon_Musk_Stempel-90)[[93]](#cite_note-96)

## Appearance and features

Main article: [List of features on X](/wiki/List_of_features_on_X "List of features on X")

### Tweets

"Twitter hashtag" redirects here. For more about hashtags, see [Hashtag](/wiki/Hashtag "Hashtag").

Main article: [Tweet (social media)](/wiki/Tweet_(social_media) "Tweet (social media)")

Further information: [Mention (blogging) § @ (at sign)](/wiki/Mention_(blogging)#@_(at_sign) "Mention (blogging)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/34/Twitter_wikipedia.png/250px-Twitter_wikipedia.png)](/wiki/File:Twitter_wikipedia.png)

The account page for Wikipedia on X, July 2025

Posts (commonly and unofficially called "tweets") are publicly visible by default, but senders can restrict message delivery to their followers. Users can mute users they do not wish to interact with, block accounts from viewing their posts, and remove accounts from their followers list.[[94]](#cite_note-97)[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-99) Users can post via the website, compatible external applications, or by [Short Message Service](/wiki/Short_Message_Service "Short Message Service") (SMS).[[97]](#cite_note-100) Users may subscribe to other users' posts—this is known as "following" and subscribers are known as "followers"[[98]](#cite_note-lists1-101) or "tweeps", a portmanteau of Twitter and peeps.[[99]](#cite_note-102) Posts can be [forwarded](/wiki/Forward_(email) "Forward (email)") by other users to their own [feed](/wiki/Web_feed "Web feed"), a process commonly called a "[retweet](/wiki/Retweet "Retweet")" (officially "repost"). In 2015, Twitter launched "quote tweet",[[100]](#cite_note-verge-quotetweet-103) a feature (now named "quote repost") which allows users to add a comment to their post, embedding one post in the other.[[101]](#cite_note-104) Users can also "[like](/wiki/Like_button "Like button")" individual tweets.[[102]](#cite_note-105)

The counters for likes, retweets, and replies appear next to the respective buttons in timelines such as on profile pages and search results. Counters for likes and reposts exist on a post's standalone page too. Since 2020, quote tweets have their own counter.[[100]](#cite_note-verge-quotetweet-103) Until the legacy desktop front end that was discontinued in 2020, a row with miniature profile pictures of up to ten liking or retweeting users was displayed, as well as a tweet reply counter next to the according button on a tweet's page.[[103]](#cite_note-106)[[104]](#cite_note-107)

Twitter allows users to update their profile via their phones either by text messaging or by apps.[[105]](#cite_note-108) Twitter announced in a tweet in 2022, that the ability to edit a tweet was being tested for select users. Eventually, all [Twitter Blue](#Blue) subscribers would be able to use the feature.[[106]](#cite_note-109) Users can group posts together by topic or type by use of [hashtags](/wiki/Hashtag "Hashtag") – words or phrases prefixed with a "`#`" sign. Similarly, the "`@`" sign followed by a [username](/wiki/Username "Username") is used for mentioning or replying to other users.[[107]](#cite_note-110) In 2014, Twitter introduced hashflags, special hashtags that automatically generate a custom [emoji](/wiki/Emoji "Emoji") next to them for a period of time.[[108]](#cite_note-111) Hashflags may be generated by Twitter themselves[[109]](#cite_note-112) or purchased by corporations.[[110]](#cite_note-113) To repost a message from another user and share it with one's own followers, a user can click the repost button within the post. Users can reply to other accounts' replies. Users can hide replies to their messages and select who can reply to each of their tweets before sending them: anyone, accounts who follow the post's author, specific accounts, or none.[[111]](#cite_note-114)[[112]](#cite_note-115)

The original, strict 140 character limit was gradually relaxed. In 2016, Twitter announced that attachments, links, and media such as photos, videos, and the person's handle, would no longer count.[[113]](#cite_note-116)[[114]](#cite_note-New_Twitter_Name-117) In 2017, Twitter handles were similarly excluded[[115]](#cite_note-118) and Twitter doubled its character limitation to 280.[[116]](#cite_note-new280limit-119) Under the new limit, [glyphs](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph") are counted as a variable number of characters, depending upon the script they are from.[[116]](#cite_note-new280limit-119) From 2023 Twitter Blue users could create posts with up to 4,000 characters in length.[[117]](#cite_note-eg-8feb2023-120)

t.co is a [URL shortening](/wiki/URL_shortening "URL shortening") service created by Twitter.[[118]](#cite_note-twitterhelpshort-121) It is only available for links posted to Twitter and not general use.[[118]](#cite_note-twitterhelpshort-121) All links posted to Twitter use a t.co wrapper.[[119]](#cite_note-twitterblog-122) Twitter intended the service to protect users from malicious sites,[[118]](#cite_note-twitterhelpshort-121) and to use it to track clicks on links within tweets.[[118]](#cite_note-twitterhelpshort-121)[[120]](#cite_note-123)

In June 2011, Twitter announced its own integrated photo-sharing service that enables users to upload a photo and attach it to a Tweet right from Twitter.com.[[121]](#cite_note-124) Users now have the ability to add pictures to Twitter's search by adding hashtags to the tweet.[[122]](#cite_note-twitpic-125) Twitter plans to provide photo galleries designed to gather and syndicate all photos that a user has uploaded on Twitter and third-party services such as [TwitPic](/wiki/TwitPic "TwitPic").[[122]](#cite_note-twitpic-125) In 2016 Twitter introduced the ability to add a caption of up to 480 characters to each image attached to a tweet,[[123]](#cite_note-hc_420-126)[[124]](#cite_note-420char-127) accessible via screen reading software or by hovering the mouse above a picture inside [TweetDeck](/wiki/TweetDeck "TweetDeck"). In 2022, Twitter made the ability to add and view captions globally available. Descriptions can be added to any uploaded image with a limit of 1000 characters. Images that have a description will feature a badge that says *ALT* in the bottom left corner, which will bring up the description when clicked.[[125]](#cite_note-AltDesc2022-128)

In 2015, Twitter began to roll out the ability to attach poll questions to tweets. Polls are open for up to 7 days, and voters are not identified.[[126]](#cite_note-129) In Twitter's early years, users could communicate with Twitter using SMS. This was discontinued in most countries in April 2020 after hackers exposed vulnerabilities.[[127]](#cite_note-130)[[128]](#cite_note-VergeDiscontinuation-131)

### Multimedia content

In 2016, Twitter began to place a larger focus on live streaming video programming, hosting events including streams of the [Republican](/wiki/2016_Republican_National_Convention "2016 Republican National Convention") and [Democratic](/wiki/2016_Democratic_National_Convention "2016 Democratic National Convention") conventions,[[129]](#cite_note-132) and winning a bid for non-exclusive streaming rights to ten [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") games in 2016.[[130]](#cite_note-verge-twitterstreamingvideo-133)[[131]](#cite_note-ars-tnftwitter-134) In 2017, Twitter announced that it planned to construct a 24-hour streaming video channel hosted within the service, featuring content from various partners.[[130]](#cite_note-verge-twitterstreamingvideo-133)[[132]](#cite_note-recode-twittervideo-135) Twitter announced a number of new and expanded partnerships for its streaming video services at the event, including [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P."), [BuzzFeed](/wiki/BuzzFeed "BuzzFeed"), [Cheddar](/wiki/Cheddar_(TV_channel) "Cheddar (TV channel)"), [IMG Fashion](/wiki/WME/IMG "WME/IMG"), [Live Nation Entertainment](/wiki/Live_Nation_Entertainment "Live Nation Entertainment"), [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"), [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") and [BET](/wiki/BET_(TV_channel) "BET (TV channel)"), [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network"), the [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour"), *[The Players' Tribune](/wiki/The_Players%27_Tribune "The Players' Tribune")*, [Ben Silverman](/wiki/Ben_Silverman "Ben Silverman") and [Howard T. Owens](/wiki/Howard_T._Owens "Howard T. Owens")' Propagate, *[The Verge](/wiki/The_Verge "The Verge")*, [Stadium](/wiki/Stadium_(sports_network) "Stadium (sports network)") and the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA").[[133]](#cite_note-variety-streamingchannel-136) as of the first quarter of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=X_(social_network)&action=edit), Twitter had over 200 content partners, who streamed over 800 hours of video over 450 events.[[133]](#cite_note-variety-streamingchannel-136)

Twitter Spaces is a [social audio](/wiki/Social_audio "Social audio") feature that enables users to host or participate in a live-audio virtual environment called *space* for conversation. A maximum of 13 people are allowed onstage. The feature was initially limited to users with at least 600 followers, but since October 2021, any Twitter user can create a Space.[[134]](#cite_note-137)

In March 2020, Twitter began to test a [stories](/wiki/Stories_(social_media) "Stories (social media)") feature known as "fleets" in some markets,[[135]](#cite_note-Perez-2020-138)[[136]](#cite_note-139) which officially launched on November 17, 2020.[[137]](#cite_note-140)[[138]](#cite_note-141) Fleets could contain text and media, are only accessible for 24 hours after they are posted, and are accessed within the Twitter app;[[135]](#cite_note-Perez-2020-138) Twitter announced it would start implementing advertising into fleets in June 2021.[[139]](#cite_note-142) Fleets were removed in August 2021; Twitter had intended for fleets to encourage more users to tweet regularly, but instead they were generally used by already-active users.[[140]](#cite_note-expiringfeature-143)

### Trending topics

Main article: [Twitter trends](/wiki/Twitter_trends "Twitter trends")

Twitter introduced its "trends" feature in mid-2008, an algorithmic lists of trending topics among users.[[141]](#cite_note-Ohlheiser2022-144) A word or phrase mentioned can become "trending topic" based on an algorithm.[[141]](#cite_note-Ohlheiser2022-144) Because a relatively small number of users can affect trending topics through a concerted campaign, the feature has been the targeted of concerted manipulation campaigns.[[141]](#cite_note-Ohlheiser2022-144) While some campaigns are innocuous, others have promoted conspiracy theories or hoaxes, or sought to amplify extremist messages.[[141]](#cite_note-Ohlheiser2022-144) Some featured trends are globally displayed, while others are limited to a specific country.[[141]](#cite_note-Ohlheiser2022-144)

A 2021 study by [EPFL](/wiki/%C3%89cole_Polytechnique_F%C3%A9d%C3%A9rale_de_Lausanne "École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne") researchers found that frequent "ephemeral [astroturfing](/wiki/Astroturfing "Astroturfing")" efforts targeted at Trends; from 2015 to 2019, "47% of local trends in Turkey and 20% of global trends are fake, created from scratch by bots...The fake trends discovered include [phishing](/wiki/Phishing "Phishing") apps, gambling promotions, disinformation campaigns, political slogans, [hate speech](/wiki/Hate_speech "Hate speech") against vulnerable populations and even marriage proposals."[[142]](#cite_note-145)[[143]](#cite_note-146) The *[MIT Technology Review](/wiki/MIT_Technology_Review "MIT Technology Review")* reported that, as of 2022, Twitter "sometimes manually overrides particularly objectionable trends" and, for some trends, used both algorithmic and human input to select representative tweets with context.[[141]](#cite_note-Ohlheiser2022-144)

### Lists

In late 2009, the "Twitter Lists" feature was added, making it possible for users to follow a curated list of accounts all at once, rather than following individual users.[[98]](#cite_note-lists1-101)[[144]](#cite_note-147) Currently,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] lists can be set to either public or private. Public lists may be recommended to users via the general Lists interface and appear in search results.[[145]](#cite_note-148) If a user follows a public list, it will appear in the "View Lists" section of their profile, so that other users may quickly find it and follow it as well.[[146]](#cite_note-149) Private lists can only be followed if the creator shares a specific link to their list. Lists add a separate tab to the Twitter interface with the title of the list, such as "News" or "Economics".

### Moments

In October 2015, Twitter introduced "Moments"—a feature that allows users to curate tweets from other users into a larger collection. Twitter initially intended the feature to be used by its in-house editorial team and other partners; they populated a dedicated tab in Twitter's apps, chronicling news headlines, sporting events, and other content.[[147]](#cite_note-150)[[148]](#cite_note-151) In September 2016, creation of moments became available to all Twitter users.[[149]](#cite_note-152)

### Algorithm

On October 21, 2021, a report based on a "long-running, massive-scale randomized experiment" that analyzed "millions of tweets sent between 1 April and 15 August 2020", found that Twitter's [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") [recommendation algorithm](/wiki/Recommendation_algorithm "Recommendation algorithm") amplified right-leaning politics on personalized user Home timelines.[[150]](#cite_note-Huszár_20211021-153): 1[[151]](#cite_note-BBC_20211022-154) The report compared seven countries with active Twitter users where data was available (Germany, Canada, the United Kingdom, Japan, France, and Spain) and examined tweets "from major political groups and politicians".[[150]](#cite_note-Huszár_20211021-153): 4 Researchers used the 2019 Chapel Hill Expert Survey (CHESDATA) to position parties on political ideology within each country.[[150]](#cite_note-Huszár_20211021-153): 4 The "machine learning algorithms", introduced by Twitter in 2016, personalized 99% of users' feeds by displaying tweets (even older tweets and retweets from accounts the user had not directly followed) that the algorithm had "deemed relevant" to the users' past preferences.[[150]](#cite_note-Huszár_20211021-153): 4 Twitter randomly chose 1% of users whose Home timelines displayed content in reverse-chronological order from users they directly followed.[[150]](#cite_note-Huszár_20211021-153): 2

In 2026, a report released in [*Nature*](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)") found X's feed algorithm promotes conservative content and deprioritizes traditional media in favor of conservative activists.[[152]](#cite_note-Gauthier_2026218-155)[[153]](#cite_note-156) Based on an analysis of 4,965 participants in 2023 over 7 weeks, it found switching from a chronological feed to an algorithmic feed increased engagement and shifted users to adopt conservative political positions, particularly around "policy priorities, perceptions of criminal investigations into Donald Trump and views on the war in Ukraine". It also found that exposure to algorithmic content led users to follow conservative political accounts and that users kept following them even after the algorithm was switched off.[[152]](#cite_note-Gauthier_2026218-155): 1

### Mobile

Twitter had mobile apps for [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone"), [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad"), and [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)").[[154]](#cite_note-157) In April 2017, Twitter introduced *Twitter Lite*, a [progressive web app](/wiki/Progressive_web_app "Progressive web app") designed for regions with unreliable and slow Internet connections, with a size of less than one [megabyte](/wiki/Megabyte "Megabyte"), designed for devices with limited storage capacity.[[155]](#cite_note-158)[[156]](#cite_note-159)

### X Premium (formerly Twitter Blue)

On June 3, 2021, Twitter announced a paid subscription service called Twitter Blue. Following [Twitter's rebranding to "X"](/wiki/Twitter_under_Elon_Musk "Twitter under Elon Musk"), the subscription service was initially renamed to X Blue (or simply Blue), and, on August 5, 2023, was rebranded as [X Premium](/wiki/X_Premium "X Premium") (or simply Premium).[[157]](#cite_note-160)[[158]](#cite_note-161) The subscription provides additional premium features to the service.[[159]](#cite_note-Perez-2021-162)[[160]](#cite_note-163) In November 2023 a "Premium+" subscription was launched, with a higher monthly fee giving benefits such as the omission of adverts on For You and Following feeds.[[161]](#cite_note-164)

#### Verification of paid accounts

Main article: [Blue check](/wiki/Blue_check "Blue check")

In November 2022, Musk announced plans to add account verification and the ability to upload longer audio and video to Twitter Blue. A previous perk offering advertising-free news articles from participating publishers was dropped, but Musk stated that Twitter did want to work with publishers on a similar "[paywall](/wiki/Paywall "Paywall") bypass" perk.[[162]](#cite_note-Roth-2022-165)[[163]](#cite_note-Peters-2022-166)[[164]](#cite_note-Roth-2022a-167) Musk had pushed for a more expensive version of Twitter Blue following his takeover, arguing that it would be needed to offset a decline in advertising revenue.[[165]](#cite_note-168) Twitter states that paid verification is required to help reduce fraudulent accounts.[[166]](#cite_note-Sawers-2023-169)

The verification marker was included in a premium tier of Twitter Blue introduced on November 9, 2022, priced at US$7.99 per month.[[167]](#cite_note-170) On November 11, 2022, after the introduction of this feature led to prominent issues involving accounts using the feature to impersonate public figures and companies, Twitter Blue with verification was temporarily suspended.[[168]](#cite_note-171)[[169]](#cite_note-Lerman-2022-172) After about a month, Twitter Blue was relaunched on December 12, 2022, though for those purchasing the service through the [iOS app store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS/iPadOS) "App Store (iOS/iPadOS)"), the cost will be $10.99 a month as to offset the 30% revenue split that Apple takes.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

Twitter initially grandfathered users and entities that had gained verification due to their status as public figures, referring to them as "legacy verified accounts" that "may or may not be notable".[[171]](#cite_note-174) On March 25, 2023, it was announced that "legacy" verification status would be removed; a subscription will be required to retain verified status, costing $1,000 per-month for organizations (which are designated with a gold verified symbol),[[166]](#cite_note-Sawers-2023-169) plus an additional $50 for each "affiliate".[[172]](#cite_note-Gans-2023-175)[[173]](#cite_note-Spangler-2023-176) The change was originally scheduled for April 1, 2023, but was delayed to April 20, 2023, following criticism of the changes.[[174]](#cite_note-177) Musk also announced plans for the "For You" timeline to prioritize verified accounts and user followers only beginning April 15, 2023, and threatened to only allow verified users to participate in polls (although the latter change has yet to occur).[[175]](#cite_note-178)

Effective April 21, 2023, Twitter requires companies to participate in the verified organizations program to purchase advertising on the platform, although companies that spend at least $1,000 on advertising per-month automatically receive membership in the program at no additional cost.[[166]](#cite_note-Sawers-2023-169) From April 25, 2023, verified users are now prioritized in replies to tweets.[[176]](#cite_note-179)[[177]](#cite_note-Kaustubh-2023-180)

### User monetization

In 2021, the company opened applications for its premium subscription options called Super Follows. This lets eligible accounts charge $2.99, $4.99 or $9.99 per month to subscribe to the account.[[178]](#cite_note-181) The launch only generated about $6,000 in its first two weeks.[[179]](#cite_note-182) In 2023, the Super Follows feature was rebranded as simply "subscriptions", allowing users to publish exclusive long-form posts and videos for their subscribers; the pivot in marketing was reportedly intended to help compete with [Substack](/wiki/Substack "Substack").[[180]](#cite_note-183)

In May 2021, Twitter began testing a Tip Jar feature on its iOS and Android clients. The feature allows users to send monetary tips to certain accounts, providing a financial incentive for content creators on the platform. The Tip Jar is optional and users can choose whether or not to enable tips for their account.[[181]](#cite_note-184) On September 23, 2021, Twitter announced that it will allow users to tip users on the social network with [bitcoin](/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin"). The feature will be available for iOS users. Previously, users could tip with fiat currency using services such as Square's [Cash App](/wiki/Cash_App "Cash App") and PayPal's [Venmo](/wiki/Venmo "Venmo"). Twitter will integrate the Strike bitcoin lightning wallet service. It was noted that at this current time, Twitter will not take a cut of any money sent through the tips feature.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

On August 27, 2021, Twitter rolled out Ticketed Spaces, which let Twitter Spaces hosts charge between $1 and $999 for access to their rooms.[[183]](#cite_note-186) In April 2022, Twitter announced that it will partner with [Stripe, Inc.](/wiki/Stripe,_Inc. "Stripe, Inc.") for piloting cryptocurrency payouts for limited users in the platform. Eligible users of Ticketed Spaces and Super Follows will be able to receive their earnings in the form of USD coin, a stablecoin whose value is that of the U.S. dollar. Users can also hold their earnings in crypto wallets, and then exchange them into other cryptocurrencies.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

### E-commerce

In July 2021, Twitter began testing a "Shop module" for iOS users in the US, allowing accounts associated with brands to display a carousel of cards on their profiles showcasing products. Unlike the Buy button, where order fulfillment was handed from within Twitter, these cards are external links to online storefronts from which the products may be purchased.[[185]](#cite_note-188) In March 2022, Twitter expanded the test to allow companies to showcase up to 50 products on their profiles.[[186]](#cite_note-189) In November 2021, Twitter introduced support for "shoppable" live streams, in which brands can hold streaming events that similarly display banners and pages highlighting products that are featured in the presentation.[[187]](#cite_note-190)

### X Money Account

In January 2025, X announced plans to introduce an "X Money Account" feature in 2025.[[188]](#cite_note-191)[[189]](#cite_note-192) The product would be a digital wallet and enable X users to move funds between traditional bank accounts and their digital wallet and make instant peer-to-peer payments.[[190]](#cite_note-193) [Visa](/wiki/Visa_Inc. "Visa Inc.") was announced as partnering with X on the project and, at least initially, [cryptocurrencies](/wiki/Cryptocurrencies "Cryptocurrencies") would not be supported.[[191]](#cite_note-194)

## Usage

Main article: [Twitter usage](/wiki/Twitter_usage "Twitter usage")

Daily user estimates vary as the company does not publish statistics on active accounts. A February 2009 [Compete.com](/wiki/Compete.com "Compete.com") blog entry ranked Twitter as the third most used social network based on their count of 6 million unique monthly visitors and 55 million monthly visits.[[49]](#cite_note-Kazeniac-51) An April 2017 a [statista.com](/wiki/Statista.com "Statista.com") blog entry ranked Twitter as the tenth most used social network based on their count of 319 million monthly visitors.[[192]](#cite_note-195) Its global user base in 2017 was 328 million.[[193]](#cite_note-196) According to Musk, the platform had 500 million monthly active users in March 2023, 550 million in March 2024, and 600 million in May 2024.[[194]](#cite_note-smt-600m-197)[[195]](#cite_note-198)[[196]](#cite_note-199)

### Demographics

See also: [Black Twitter](/wiki/Black_Twitter "Black Twitter")

In 2009, Twitter was mainly used by older adults who might not have used other social sites before Twitter.[[197]](#cite_note-teensdonttweet-200) According to [comScore](/wiki/ComScore "ComScore") only 11% of Twitter's users were aged 12 to 17.[[197]](#cite_note-teensdonttweet-200) According to a study by [Sysomos](/wiki/Sysomos "Sysomos") in June 2009, women made up a slightly larger Twitter demographic than men—53% over 47%. It also stated that 5% of users accounted for 75% of all activity.[[198]](#cite_note-201) According to [Quantcast](/wiki/Quantcast "Quantcast"), 27 million people in the US used Twitter in September 2009; 63% of Twitter users were under 35 years old; 60% of Twitter users were Caucasian, but a higher than average (compared to other Internet properties) were African American/black (16%) and Hispanic (11%); 58% of Twitter users have a total household income of at least US$60,000.[[199]](#cite_note-202) The prevalence of African American Twitter usage and in many popular hashtags has been the subject of research studies.[[200]](#cite_note-203)[[201]](#cite_note-204)

Twitter grew from 100 million monthly active users (MAUs) in September 2011,[[202]](#cite_note-205) to 255 million in March 2014,[[203]](#cite_note-investor.twitterinc.com-206) and more than 330 million in early 2019.[[204]](#cite_note-207)[[205]](#cite_note-208)[[62]](#cite_note-Twitter-65) In 2013, there were over 100 million users actively using Twitter daily and about 500 million tweets every day.[[206]](#cite_note-209) A 2016 [Pew research poll](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") found that Twitter is used by 24% of all online US adults. It was equally popular with men and women (24% and 25% of online Americans respectively), but more popular with younger generations (36% of 18–29-year olds).[[207]](#cite_note-210) A 2019 survey conducted by the [Pew Foundation](/wiki/The_Pew_Charitable_Trusts "The Pew Charitable Trusts") found that Twitter users are three times as likely to be younger than 50 years old, with the median age of adult U.S. users being 40. The survey found that 10% of users who are most active on Twitter are responsible for 80% of all tweets.[[208]](#cite_note-211)

### Content

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Content_of_Tweets.svg/250px-Content_of_Tweets.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Content_of_Tweets.svg)

Content of tweets according to Pear Analytics in August 2009

News (3.6%)

Spam (3.8%)

Self-promotion (6%)

Pointless babble (40%)

Conversational (38%)

Pass-along value (8.7%)

[San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio")-based market-research firm Pear Analytics analyzed 2,000 tweets (originating from the United States and in English) over a two-week period in August 2009 from 11:00 am to 5:00 pm (CST) and separated them into six categories.[[209]](#cite_note-212) [Pointless babble](/wiki/Small_talk "Small talk") made up 40%, with 38% being conversational. Pass-along value had 9%, self-promotion 6% with [spam](/wiki/Spam_(electronic) "Spam (electronic)") and news each making 4%.

Despite Jack Dorsey's own open contention that a message on Twitter is "a short burst of inconsequential information", social networking researcher [danah boyd](/wiki/Danah_boyd "Danah boyd") responded to the Pear Analytics survey by arguing that what the Pear researchers labeled "pointless babble" is better characterized as "[social grooming](/wiki/Social_grooming "Social grooming")" or "peripheral awareness" (which she justifies as persons "want[ing] to know what the people around them are thinking and doing and feeling, even when co-presence isn't viable").[[210]](#cite_note-213) Similarly, a survey of Twitter users found that a more specific social role of passing along messages that include a hyperlink is an expectation of reciprocal linking by followers.[[211]](#cite_note-214)

### Levels of use and class action lawsuit

According to research published in April 2014, around 44% of user accounts have never tweeted.[[212]](#cite_note-215) About 22% of Americans say they have used Twitter, according to a 2019 [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") survey.[[213]](#cite_note-216) In 2009, [Nielsen Online](/wiki/Nielsen_Online "Nielsen Online") reported that Twitter had a user-retention rate of 40%. Many people stop using the service after a month; therefore the site may potentially reach only about 10% of all [Internet users](/wiki/Global_internet_usage "Global internet usage").[[214]](#cite_note-217) Noting how demographics of Twitter users differ from the average Americans, commentators have cautioned against media narratives that treat Twitter as representative of the population,[[215]](#cite_note-218) adding that only 10% of users Tweet actively, and that 90% of Twitter users have Tweeted no more than twice. In 2016, shareholders sued Twitter, alleging it "artificially inflated its stock price by misleading them about user engagement". The company announced on September 20, 2021, that it would pay $809.5 million to settle this class-action lawsuit.[[216]](#cite_note-219)

### Decline on mobile devices

As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=X_(social_network)&action=edit), usage of X on mobile devices has been in a progressive decline. At the same time, mobile usage of the rival social network [Threads](/wiki/Threads_(social_network) "Threads (social network)") has been increasing. In January 2026, Threads usage finally rose to match that of X. X still remains the dominant network on non-mobile traffic.[[217]](#cite_note-220)

## Branding

Before its rebranding to X, Twitter was internationally identifiable by its signature bird logo, or the Twitter Bird. The original logo, which was simply the word *Twitter*, was in use from its launch in March 2006. It was accompanied by an image of a bird which was later discovered to be a piece of [clip art](/wiki/Clip_art "Clip art") created by the British graphic designer [Simon Oxley](/wiki/Simon_Oxley "Simon Oxley").[[218]](#cite_note-nytimes_logo-221) A new logo had to be redesigned by founder Biz Stone with help from designer Philip Pascuzzo, which resulted in a more cartoon-like bird in 2009. This version had been named "Larry the Bird" after [Larry Bird](/wiki/Larry_Bird "Larry Bird") of the NBA's [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") fame.[[218]](#cite_note-nytimes_logo-221)[[219]](#cite_note-222)

Within a year, the Larry the Bird logo underwent a redesign by Stone and Pascuzzo to eliminate the cartoon features, leaving a solid silhouette of Larry the Bird that was used from 2010 through 2012.[[218]](#cite_note-nytimes_logo-221) In 2012, Douglas Bowman created a further simplified version of Larry the Bird, keeping the solid silhouette but making it more similar to a [mountain bluebird](/wiki/Mountain_bluebird "Mountain bluebird").[[220]](#cite_note-223) This logo was simply called the "Twitter Bird" and was used until July 2023.[[218]](#cite_note-nytimes_logo-221)[[221]](#cite_note-224)[[222]](#cite_note-independent2-225)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/X_Profile_on_X_Website_while_logged_out.png/250px-X_Profile_on_X_Website_while_logged_out.png)](/wiki/File:X_Profile_on_X_Website_while_logged_out.png)

X's profile in August 2025

On July 22, 2023, Elon Musk announced that the service would be rebranded to "X",[[223]](#cite_note-226) in his pursuit of creating an "[everything app](/wiki/Super-app "Super-app")".[[222]](#cite_note-independent2-225) Musk's profile picture, along with the platform's official accounts, and the icons when browsing/signing up for the platform, were updated to reflect the new logo.[[224]](#cite_note-227) The logo resembles the Unicode [mathematical alphanumeric symbol](/wiki/Mathematical_Alphanumeric_Symbols "Mathematical Alphanumeric Symbols") U+1D54F 𝕏 [MATHEMATICAL DOUBLE-STRUCK CAPITAL X](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%9D%95%8F "wikt:𝕏") for the letter "X" styled in [double-strike bold](/wiki/Blackboard_bold "Blackboard bold").[[225]](#cite_note-:16-228)[[226]](#cite_note-229)[[227]](#cite_note-230)

Mike Proulx of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* was critical of this change, saying the brand value has been "wiped out". Mike Carr says the new logo gives a "'[Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(Nineteen_Eighty-Four) "Big Brother (Nineteen Eighty-Four)")' tech overlord vibe" in contrast to the "cuddly" nature of the previous bird logo.[[228]](#cite_note-231) Users [review bombed](/wiki/Review_bomb "Review bomb") the newly rebranded "X" app on the [iOS App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS/iPadOS) "App Store (iOS/iPadOS)") on the day it was revealed, and *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*'s Miles Klee said that the rebrand "reeks of desperation".[[229]](#cite_note-232)[[230]](#cite_note-233)

The startup Operation Bluebird requested to the [United States Patent and Trademark Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office") to cancel the existing Twitter trademarks and open a new one for itself, planning to develop a new page that would restore the name, logos, and page structure of Twitter. They argued that X Corp. may have abandoned the trademark, by not using it commercially. X Corp. started a legal case against them, arguing that the trademark is still valid and enforceable, and that the new site would constitute a [trademark infringement](/wiki/Trademark_infringement "Trademark infringement"). This started the legal dispute [X Corp. v. Operation Bluebird](/wiki/X_Corp._v._Operation_Bluebird "X Corp. v. Operation Bluebird").[[231]](#cite_note-234)
