## Finances

### Revenue sources

On April 13, 2010, Twitter announced plans to offer paid advertising for companies that would be able to purchase "promoted tweets" to appear in selective search results on the Twitter website, similar to [Google Adwords'](/wiki/Adwords "Adwords") advertising model.[[232]](#cite_note-235)[[233]](#cite_note-236) Users' photos can generate royalty-free revenue for Twitter, and an agreement with [World Entertainment News Network](/wiki/World_Entertainment_News_Network "World Entertainment News Network") (WENN) was announced in May 2011.[[234]](#cite_note-237) Twitter generated an estimated US$139.5 million in advertising sales during 2011.[[235]](#cite_note-238)

In June 2011, Twitter announced that it would offer small businesses a self-service advertising system.[[236]](#cite_note-239) The self-service advertising platform was launched in March 2012 to [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express") card members and merchants in the U.S. on an invite-only basis.[[237]](#cite_note-Zach-240) To continue their advertising campaign, Twitter announced on March 20, 2012, that promoted tweets would be introduced to mobile devices.[[238]](#cite_note-241) In April 2013, Twitter announced that its Twitter Ads self-service platform, consisting of promoted tweets and promoted accounts, was available to all U.S. users without an invite.[[237]](#cite_note-Zach-240)

On August 3, 2016, Twitter launched Instant Unlock Card, a new feature that encourages people to tweet about a brand to earn rewards and use the social media network's conversational ads. The format itself consists of images or videos with call-to-action buttons and a customizable hashtag.[[239]](#cite_note-242)

### Advertising bans

In October 2017, Twitter banned the Russian media outlets [RT](/wiki/RT_(TV_network) "RT (TV network)") and [Sputnik](/wiki/Sputnik_(news_agency) "Sputnik (news agency)") from advertising on their website following the conclusions of the U.S. national intelligence report the previous January that both Sputnik and RT had been used as vehicles for [Russia's interference in the 2016 US presidential election](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_United_States_elections "Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections").[[240]](#cite_note-243) [Maria Zakharova](/wiki/Maria_Zakharova "Maria Zakharova") for the [Russian foreign ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Russia) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Russia)") said the ban was a "gross violation" by the US of free speech.[[241]](#cite_note-244)

In October 2019, Twitter announced it would stop running political ads on its ad platform effective November 22. This resulted from several spurious claims made by political ads. Company CEO Dorsey clarified that Internet advertising had great power and was extremely effective for commercial advertisers, the power brings significant risks to politics where crucial decisions impact millions of lives.[[242]](#cite_note-245) The company reversed the ban in August 2023,[[243]](#cite_note-246) publishing criteria governing political advertising which do not allow the promotion of false or misleading content, and requiring advertisers to comply with laws, with compliance being the sole responsibility of the advertiser.[[244]](#cite_note-247)

In April 2022, Twitter announced a ban on "misleading" advertisements that go against "the scientific consensus on climate change". While the company did not give full guidelines, it stated that the decisions would be made with the help of "authoritative sources", including the [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](/wiki/United_Nations_Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "United Nations Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change").[[245]](#cite_note-248)

### Coerced advertising

A 2025 article in [The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal") reported that [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon "Verizon"), [Ralph Lauren Corporation](/wiki/Ralph_Lauren_Corporation "Ralph Lauren Corporation"), and at least four other companies signed advertising contracts with X following [legal threats](/wiki/Twitter#Court_cases,_lawsuits,_and_adjudication "Twitter") from Musk and CEO [Linda Yaccarino](/wiki/Linda_Yaccarino "Linda Yaccarino").[[246]](#cite_note-249)

### Fines

X has been fined several times for non-compliance with laws and regulations. On May 25, 2022, Twitter was fined $150 million by the [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") and the United States Department of Justice for collecting users' contact details and using them for targeted advertising.[[247]](#cite_note-250)[[248]](#cite_note-251)

In December 2025, the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") fined X €120 million for alleged non-compliance with requirements of the [Digital Services Act](/wiki/Digital_Services_Act "Digital Services Act").[[249]](#cite_note-252) Days later, X banned the European Commission from advertising on the platform.[[250]](#cite_note-253)

## Technology

### Implementation

X relies on [open-source software](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software").[[251]](#cite_note-zdnet_vaughannichols_2012-254) The X web interface uses the [Ruby on Rails](/wiki/Ruby_on_Rails "Ruby on Rails") framework,[[252]](#cite_note-255) deployed on a performance enhanced [Ruby Enterprise Edition](/wiki/Ruby_Enterprise_Edition "Ruby Enterprise Edition") implementation of [Ruby](/wiki/Ruby_(programming_language) "Ruby (programming language)").[[253]](#cite_note-256)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

In the early days of Twitter, tweets were stored in [MySQL](/wiki/MySQL "MySQL") databases that were temporally [sharded](/wiki/Shard_(database_architecture) "Shard (database architecture)") (large databases were split based on time of posting). After the huge volume of tweets coming in caused problems reading from and writing to these databases, the company decided that the system needed re-engineering.[[254]](#cite_note-Krikorian-2013-257)

From Spring 2007 to 2008, the messages were handled by a Ruby [persistent](/wiki/Persistent_data_structure "Persistent data structure") queue server called Starling.[[255]](#cite_note-258) Since 2009, implementation has been gradually replaced with software written in [Scala](/wiki/Scala_(programming_language) "Scala (programming language)").[[256]](#cite_note-259) The switch from Ruby to Scala and the [JVM](/wiki/Java_Virtual_Machine "Java Virtual Machine") has given Twitter a performance boost from 200 to 300 requests per second per host to around 10,000–20,000 requests per second per host. This boost was greater than the 10x improvement that Twitter's engineers envisioned when starting the switch. The continued development of Twitter has also involved a switch from monolithic development of a single app to an architecture where different services are built independently and joined through [remote procedure calls](/wiki/Remote_procedure_call "Remote procedure call").[[254]](#cite_note-Krikorian-2013-257)

As of April 6, 2011, Twitter engineers confirmed that they had switched away from their Ruby on Rails search stack to a [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") server they call Blender.[[257]](#cite_note-Twitter_coding-260) Individual tweets are registered under unique IDs called [snowflakes](/wiki/Snowflake_ID "Snowflake ID"), and geolocation data is added using 'Rockdove'. The URL shortener *[t.co](/wiki/T.co "T.co")* then checks for a [spam](/wiki/Messaging_spam "Messaging spam") link and shortens the URL. Next, the tweets are stored in a MySQL database using [Gizzard](/wiki/Gizzard_(Scala_framework) "Gizzard (Scala framework)"), and the user receives an acknowledgement that the tweets were sent. Tweets are then sent to search engines via the Firehose API. The process is managed by [FlockDB](/wiki/FlockDB "FlockDB") and takes an average of 350 ms.[[251]](#cite_note-zdnet_vaughannichols_2012-254)

On August 16, 2013, [Raffi Krikorian](/wiki/Raffi_Krikorian "Raffi Krikorian"), Twitter's vice president of platform engineering, shared in a blog post that the company's infrastructure handled almost 143,000 tweets per second during that week, setting a new record. Krikorian explained that Twitter achieved this record by blending its homegrown and open source technologies.[[254]](#cite_note-Krikorian-2013-257)[[258]](#cite_note-261)

### API and developer platform

The service's API allows other [web services](/wiki/Web_service "Web service") and applications to integrate with Twitter.[[259]](#cite_note-262) Developer interest in Twitter began immediately following its launch, prompting the company to release the first version of its public API in September 2006.[[260]](#cite_note-263) The API quickly became iconic as a reference implementation for public [REST APIs](/wiki/REST_API "REST API") and is widely cited in programming tutorials.[[261]](#cite_note-264)

From 2006 until 2010, Twitter's developer platform experienced strong growth and a highly favorable reputation. Developers built upon the public API to create the first Twitter mobile phone clients as well as the first URL shortener. Between 2010 and 2012, however, Twitter made a number of decisions that were received unfavorably by the developer community.[[262]](#cite_note-265) In 2010, Twitter mandated that all developers adopt [OAuth](/wiki/OAuth "OAuth") authentication with just 9 weeks of notice.[[263]](#cite_note-266) Later that year, Twitter launched its own URL shortener, in direct competition with some of its most well-known third-party developers.[[264]](#cite_note-267) And in 2012, Twitter introduced stricter usage limits for its API, "completely crippling" some developers.[[265]](#cite_note-268)[[266]](#cite_note-269) While these moves successfully increased the stability and security of the service, they were broadly perceived as hostile to developers, causing them to lose trust in the platform.[[267]](#cite_note-270)

In July 2020, Twitter released version 2.0 of the public API[[268]](#cite_note-271) and began showcasing Twitter apps made by third-party developers on its Twitter Toolbox section in April 2022.[[269]](#cite_note-272)

In January 2023, Twitter ended third-party access to its APIs, forcing all third-party Twitter clients to shut down.[[270]](#cite_note-273) This was controversial among the developer community, as many third-party apps predated the company's official apps, and the change was not announced beforehand. Twitterrific's Sean Heber confirmed in a blog post that the 16-year-old app has been discontinued. "We are sorry to say that the app's sudden and undignified demise is due to an unannounced and undocumented policy change by an increasingly capricious Twitter – a Twitter that we no longer recognize as trustworthy nor want to work with any longer."[[271]](#cite_note-274) In February 2023, Twitter announced it would be ending free access to Twitter API, and began offering paid tier plans with a more limited access.[[272]](#cite_note-275)

### Innovator's patent agreement

On April 17, 2012, Twitter announced it would implement an "Innovators Patent Agreement" which would obligate Twitter to only use its patents for defensive purposes.[*[clarify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[273]](#cite_note-276)

### Open source

Twitter has a history of both using and releasing [open-source software](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") while overcoming technical challenges of their service.[[274]](#cite_note-277) A page in their developer documentation thanks dozens of open-source projects which they have used, from [revision control](/wiki/Revision_control "Revision control") software like [Git](/wiki/Git_(software) "Git (software)") to programming languages such as Ruby and Scala.[[275]](#cite_note-278) Software released as open source by the company includes the [Gizzard Scala framework](/wiki/Gizzard_(Scala_framework) "Gizzard (Scala framework)") for creating distributed datastores, the distributed graph database [FlockDB](/wiki/FlockDB "FlockDB"), the Finagle library for building asynchronous [RPC](/wiki/Remote_procedure_call "Remote procedure call") servers and clients, the TwUI [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") framework for [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS"), and the Bower client-side package manager.[[276]](#cite_note-279) The popular [Bootstrap frontend framework](/wiki/Bootstrap_(front-end_framework) "Bootstrap (front-end framework)") was also started at Twitter and is 10th most popular repository on [GitHub](/wiki/GitHub "GitHub").[[277]](#cite_note-280)

On March 31, 2023, Twitter released the [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code") for Twitter's recommendation [algorithm](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm"),[[278]](#cite_note-281) which determines what tweets show up on the user's personal timeline, to [GitHub](/wiki/GitHub "GitHub"). According to Twitter's blog post: "We believe that we have a responsibility, as the town square of the internet, to make our platform transparent. So today we are taking the first step in a new era of transparency and opening much of our source code to the global community."[[279]](#cite_note-282) [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"), the CEO at the time, had been promising the move for a while – on March 24, 2022, before he owned the site, he polled his followers about whether Twitter's algorithm should be open source, and around 83% of the responses said "yes". In February, he promised it would happen within a week before pushing back the deadline to March 31.[[280]](#cite_note-283) Twitter updated its source code repository in January 2026 with a new algorithm that depends on an external [large language model](/wiki/Large_language_model "Large language model") (such as Grok) to evaluate posts, leading researchers to describe the disclosed source code as lacking in transparency.[[281]](#cite_note-284)

Also in March 2023, Twitter suffered a security attack which resulted in proprietary code being released. Twitter then had the leaked source code removed.[[282]](#cite_note-285)

### Interface

Twitter introduced the first major redesign of its user interface in September 2010, adopting a dual-pane layout with a navigation bar along the top of the screen, and an increased focus on the inline embedding of multimedia content. Critics considered the redesign an attempt to emulate features and experiences found in mobile apps and third-party Twitter clients.[[283]](#cite_note-286)[[284]](#cite_note-287)[[285]](#cite_note-288)[[286]](#cite_note-289)

The new layout was revised in 2011 with a focus on continuity with the web and mobile versions, introducing "Connect" (interactions with other users such as replies) and "Discover" (further information regarding trending topics and news headlines) tabs, an updated profile design, and moving all content to the right pane (leaving the left pane dedicated to functions and the trending topics list).[[287]](#cite_note-290) In March 2012, Twitter became available in Arabic, [Farsi](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), the first right-to-left language versions of the site.[[288]](#cite_note-291) In 2023 the Twitter Web site listed 34 languages supported by Twitter.com.[[289]](#cite_note-292)

In September 2012, a new layout for profiles was introduced, with larger "covers" that could be customized with a custom header image, and a display of the user's recent photos posted.[[290]](#cite_note-293) The "Discover" tab was discontinued in April 2015,[[291]](#cite_note-294) and was succeeded on the mobile app by an "Explore" tab—which features trending topics and moments.[[292]](#cite_note-295) In September 2018, Twitter began to migrate selected web users to its [progressive web app](/wiki/Progressive_web_applications "Progressive web applications") (based on its Twitter Lite experience for mobile web), reducing the interface to two columns. Migrations to this iteration of Twitter increased in April 2019, with some users receiving it with a modified layout.[[293]](#cite_note-296)[[294]](#cite_note-297)

In July 2019, Twitter officially released this redesign, with no further option to opt-out while logged in. It is designed to further-unify Twitter's user experience between the web and [mobile application](/wiki/Mobile_application "Mobile application") versions, adopting a three-column layout with a sidebar containing links to common areas (including "Explore" that has been merged with the search page) which previously appeared in a horizontal top bar, profile elements such as picture and header images and biography texts merged into the same column as the timeline, and features from the mobile version (such as multi-account support, and an opt-out for the "top tweets" mode on the timeline).[[295]](#cite_note-298)[[296]](#cite_note-299)

### Security

In response to early Twitter security breaches, the United States [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") (FTC) brought charges against the service; the charges were settled on June 24, 2010. This was the first time the FTC had taken action against a social network for security lapses. The settlement requires Twitter to take a number of steps to secure users' private information, including maintenance of a "comprehensive information security program" to be independently audited biannually.[[297]](#cite_note-300) After a number of high-profile hacks of official accounts, including those of the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") and *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*,[[298]](#cite_note-301) in April 2013, Twitter announced a two-factor login verification as an added measure against hacking.[[299]](#cite_note-302)

On July 15, 2020, a [major hack of Twitter](/wiki/2020_Twitter_bitcoin_scam "2020 Twitter bitcoin scam") affected 130 high-profile accounts, both verified and unverified ones such as [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), [Bill Gates](/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates"), and [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"); the hack allowed [bitcoin](/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin") scammers to send tweets via the compromised accounts that asked the followers to send bitcoin to a given public address, with the promise to double their money.[[300]](#cite_note-Statt-303) Within a few hours, Twitter disabled tweeting and reset passwords from all verified accounts.[[300]](#cite_note-Statt-303) Analysis of the event revealed that the scammers had used [social engineering](/wiki/Social_engineering_(security) "Social engineering (security)") to obtain credentials from Twitter employees to access an administration tool used by Twitter to view and change these accounts' personal details as to gain access as part of a "[smash and grab](/wiki/Smash_and_grab "Smash and grab")" attempt to make money quickly, with an estimated US$120,000 in bitcoin deposited in various accounts before Twitter intervened.[[301]](#cite_note-304) Several law enforcement entities including the FBI launched investigations into the attack.[[302]](#cite_note-305)

On August 5, 2022, Twitter disclosed that a bug introduced in a June 2021 update to the service allowed threat actors to link email addresses and phone numbers to twitter user's accounts.[[303]](#cite_note-306)[[304]](#cite_note-307) The bug was reported through Twitter's [bug bounty program](/wiki/Bug_bounty_program "Bug bounty program") in January 2022 and subsequently fixed. While Twitter originally believed no one had taken advantage of the vulnerability, it was later revealed that a user on the online hacking forum [Breach Forums](/wiki/BreachForums "BreachForums") had used the vulnerability to compile a list of over 5.4 million user profiles, which they offered to sell for $30,000.[[305]](#cite_note-308)[[306]](#cite_note-309) The information compiled by the hacker includes user's screen names, location and email addresses which could be used in [phishing](/wiki/Phishing "Phishing") attacks or used to deanonymize accounts running under pseudonyms.

### Outages

During an outage, Twitter users were at one time shown the "fail whale" [error message](/wiki/Error_message "Error message") image created by [Yiying Lu](/wiki/Yiying_Lu "Yiying Lu"),[[307]](#cite_note-310) illustrating eight orange birds using a net to hoist a whale from the ocean captioned "Too many tweets! Please wait a moment and try again."[[308]](#cite_note-311) Web designer and Twitter user [Jen Simmons](/wiki/Jen_Simmons "Jen Simmons") was the first to coin the term "fail whale" in a September 2007 tweet.[[309]](#cite_note-312)[[310]](#cite_note-313) In a November 2013 *Wired* interview Chris Fry, VP of Engineering at that time, noted that the company had taken the "fail whale" out of use as the platform was now more stable.[[311]](#cite_note-314) Twitter had approximately 98% [uptime](/wiki/Uptime "Uptime") in 2007 (or about six full days of downtime).[[312]](#cite_note-315) The downtime was particularly noticeable during events popular with the technology industry such as the 2008 [Macworld Conference & Expo](/wiki/Macworld_Conference_%26_Expo "Macworld Conference & Expo") keynote address.[[313]](#cite_note-316)[[314]](#cite_note-downtime4-317) As of January 16, 2026, Twitter's error message during an outage is "Something went wrong, but don't fret – Let's give it another shot."[[315]](#cite_note-318)

## User accounts

### Verified accounts

Main article: [Blue check](/wiki/Blue_check "Blue check")

In June 2009, after being criticized by [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West") and sued by [Tony La Russa](/wiki/Tony_La_Russa "Tony La Russa") over unauthorized accounts run by [impersonators](/wiki/Impersonator "Impersonator"), the company launched their "Verified Accounts" program.[[316]](#cite_note-319)[[317]](#cite_note-verifiedhistory-320) Twitter stated that an account with a "blue tick" verification badge indicates "we've been in contact with the person or entity the account is representing and verified that it is approved".[[318]](#cite_note-verifiedlaunch-321) In July 2016, Twitter announced a public application process to grant verified status to an account "if it is determined to be of public interest" and that verification "does not imply an endorsement".[[319]](#cite_note-322)[[320]](#cite_note-323)[[321]](#cite_note-324) Verified status allows access to some features unavailable to other users, such as only seeing mentions from other verified accounts.[[322]](#cite_note-325)

In November 2020, Twitter announced a relaunch of its verification system in 2021. According to the new policy, Twitter verifies six different types of accounts; for three of them (companies, brands, and influential individuals like activists), the existence of a Wikipedia page will be one criterion for showing that the account has "Off Twitter Notability".[[323]](#cite_note-326) Twitter states that it will re-open public verification applications at some point in "early 2021".[[324]](#cite_note-327)

In October 2022, after the takeover of Twitter by Elon Musk, it was reported that verification would instead be included in the paid Twitter Blue service, and that existing verified accounts would lose their status if they do not subscribe.[[325]](#cite_note-328) On November 1, Musk confirmed that verification would be included in Blue in the future, dismissing the existing verification system as a "lords & peasants system".[[162]](#cite_note-Roth-2022-165)[[163]](#cite_note-Peters-2022-166)[[164]](#cite_note-Roth-2022a-167) After concerns over the possibility of impersonation, Twitter subsequently reimplemented a second "Official" marker, consisting of a grey tick and "Official" text displayed under the username, for high-profile accounts of "government and commercial entities".[[326]](#cite_note-329)[[327]](#cite_note-330) In December 2022, the "Official" text was replaced by a gold checkmark for organizations, as well as a grey check mark for government and multilateral accounts.[[328]](#cite_note-Schroeder-2023-331)[[329]](#cite_note-Wiggers-2022-332)

In March 2023, the gold check mark was made available for organizations to purchase through the Verified Organizations program (formerly called Twitter Blue for Business).[[328]](#cite_note-Schroeder-2023-331)[[329]](#cite_note-Wiggers-2022-332)

### Privacy

Tweets are public, but users can also send private "direct messages".[[330]](#cite_note-Rushe-333) Information about who has chosen to follow an account and who a user has chosen to follow is also public, though accounts can be changed to "protected" which limits this information (and all tweets) to approved followers.[[331]](#cite_note-334) Twitter collects [personally identifiable information](/wiki/Personally_identifiable_information "Personally identifiable information") about its users and shares it with third parties as specified in its [privacy policy](/wiki/Privacy_policy "Privacy policy"). The service also reserves the right to sell this information as an asset if the company changes hands.[[332]](#cite_note-335)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*][[333]](#cite_note-336) Advertisers can [target users](/wiki/Behavioral_targeting "Behavioral targeting") based on their history of tweets and may quote tweets in ads[[334]](#cite_note-337) directed specifically to the user.

Twitter launched the [beta version](/wiki/Beta_version#Beta "Beta version") of their "Verified Accounts" service on June 11, 2009, allowing people with public profiles to announce their account name. The profile pages of these accounts display a badge indicating their status.[[335]](#cite_note-338) On December 14, 2010, the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") issued a [subpoena directing Twitter](/wiki/Twitter_subpoena "Twitter subpoena") to provide information for accounts registered to or associated with [WikiLeaks](/wiki/WikiLeaks "WikiLeaks").[[336]](#cite_note-339) Twitter decided to notify its users and said, "... it's our policy to notify users about law enforcement and governmental requests for their information, unless we are prevented by law from doing so."[[330]](#cite_note-Rushe-333)

In May 2011, a [claimant](/wiki/Claimant "Claimant") known as "CTB" in the case of *[CTB v Twitter Inc.](/wiki/CTB_v_News_Group_Newspapers "CTB v News Group Newspapers")* took action against Twitter at the [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") of England and Wales,[[337]](#cite_note-340) requesting that the company release details of account holders. This followed gossip posted on Twitter about professional footballer [Ryan Giggs](/wiki/Ryan_Giggs "Ryan Giggs")'s private life. This led to the [2011 British privacy injunctions controversy](/wiki/2011_British_privacy_injunctions_controversy "2011 British privacy injunctions controversy") and the "super-injunction".[[338]](#cite_note-341) Tony Wang, the head of Twitter in Europe, said that people who do "bad things" on the site would need to defend themselves under the laws of their own jurisdiction in the event of controversy and that the site would hand over information about users to the authorities when it was legally required to do so.[[339]](#cite_note-wang-342) He also suggested that Twitter would accede to a UK court order to divulge names of users responsible for "illegal activity" on the site.[[340]](#cite_note-independent-343)

Twitter acquired [Dasient](/wiki/Dasient "Dasient"), a startup that offers malware protection for businesses, in January 2012. Twitter announced plans to use Dasient to help remove hateful advertisers on the website.[[341]](#cite_note-344) Twitter also offered a feature which would allow tweets to be removed selectively by country, before deleted tweets used to be removed in all countries.[[342]](#cite_note-BBC_censor-345)[[343]](#cite_note-346) The first use of the policy was to block the account of German [neo-Nazi](/wiki/Neo-Nazi "Neo-Nazi") group [Besseres Hannover](/wiki/Besseres_Hannover "Besseres Hannover") on October 18, 2012.[[344]](#cite_note-347) The policy was used again the following day to remove [anti-Semitic](/wiki/Anti-Semitic "Anti-Semitic") French tweets with the hashtag #unbonjuif ("a good Jew").[[345]](#cite_note-348) After the sharing of images showing the killing of American journalist [James Foley](/wiki/James_Foley_(journalist) "James Foley (journalist)") in 2014, Twitter said that in certain cases it would delete pictures of people who had died after requests from family members and "authorized individuals".[[346]](#cite_note-349)[[347]](#cite_note-350)

In 2015, following updated terms of service and privacy policy, Twitter users outside the United States were legally served by the Ireland-based Twitter International Company instead of Twitter, Inc. The change made these users subject to Irish and [European Union data protection laws](/wiki/Data_Protection_Directive "Data Protection Directive").[[348]](#cite_note-irishtimes-twitterint-351) On April 8, 2020, Twitter announced that users outside of the European Economic Area or United Kingdom (thus subject to GDPR) will no longer be allowed to opt out of sharing "mobile app advertising measurements" to Twitter third-party partners.[[349]](#cite_note-352)

On October 9, 2020, Twitter took additional steps to counter misleading campaigns ahead of the 2020 US Election. Twitter's new temporary update encouraged users to "add their own commentary" before retweeting a tweet, by making 'quoting tweet' a mandatory feature instead of optional. The social network giant aimed at generating context and encouraging the circulation of more thoughtful content.[[350]](#cite_note-353) After limited results, the company ended this experiment in December 2020.[[351]](#cite_note-354)

On May 25, 2022, Twitter was fined $150 million for collecting users' phone numbers and email addresses used for [security](/wiki/Two-factor_authentication "Two-factor authentication") and using them for [targeted advertising](/wiki/Targeted_advertising "Targeted advertising"), required to notify its users, and banned from profiting from "deceptively collected data".[[352]](#cite_note-355) The [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") (FTC) and the [Department of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_justice "Ministry of justice") stated that Twitter violated a 2011 agreement not to use personal security data for targeted advertising.

In September 2024, the FTC released a report summarizing 9 company responses (including from X) to orders made by the agency pursuant to Section 6(b) of the [Federal Trade Commission Act of 1914](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission_Act_of_1914 "Federal Trade Commission Act of 1914") to provide information about user and non-user data collection (including of children and teenagers) and data use by the companies that found that the companies' user and non-user data practices put individuals vulnerable to [identity theft](/wiki/Identity_theft "Identity theft"), [stalking](/wiki/Stalking "Stalking"), unlawful discrimination, emotional distress and [mental health issues](/wiki/Digital_media_use_and_mental_health "Digital media use and mental health"), social stigma, and reputational harm.[[353]](#cite_note-356)[[354]](#cite_note-357)[[355]](#cite_note-358)

### Harassment

In August 2013, Twitter announced plans to introduce a "report abuse" button for all versions of the site following uproar, including a petition with 100,000 signatures, over Tweets that included rape and death threats to historian [Mary Beard](/wiki/Mary_Beard_(classicist) "Mary Beard (classicist)"), feminist campaigner [Caroline Criado-Perez](/wiki/Caroline_Criado-Perez "Caroline Criado-Perez") and the member of parliament [Stella Creasy](/wiki/Stella_Creasy "Stella Creasy").[[356]](#cite_note-359)[[357]](#cite_note-360)[[358]](#cite_note-361) Twitter announced new reporting and blocking policies in December 2014,[[359]](#cite_note-362)[[360]](#cite_note-363)[[361]](#cite_note-364)[[362]](#cite_note-365) including a blocking mechanism devised by [Randi Harper](/wiki/Randi_Harper "Randi Harper"), a target of [GamerGate](/wiki/GamerGate "GamerGate").[[363]](#cite_note-366)[[364]](#cite_note-367)[[365]](#cite_note-368) In February 2015, CEO [Dick Costolo](/wiki/Dick_Costolo "Dick Costolo") said he was 'frankly ashamed' at how poorly Twitter handled [trolling](/wiki/Internet_troll "Internet troll") and abuse, and admitted Twitter had lost users as a result.[[366]](#cite_note-369) As per a research study conducted by IT for Change on abuse and misogynistic trolling on Twitter directed at Indian women in public-political life, women perceived to be ideologically left-leaning, dissenters, Muslim women, political dissenters, and political commentators and women from opposition parties received a disproportionate amount of abusive and hateful messages on Twitter.[[367]](#cite_note-370)

In 2016, Twitter announced the creation of the Twitter Trust & Safety Council to help "ensure that people feel safe expressing themselves on Twitter". The council's inaugural members included 50 organizations and individuals.[[368]](#cite_note-371) The announcement of Twitter's "Trust & Safety Council" was met with objection from parts of its userbase.[[369]](#cite_note-372)[[370]](#cite_note-373) Critics accused the member organizations of being heavily skewed towards "the restriction of hate speech" and a *[Reason](/wiki/Reason_(magazine) "Reason (magazine)")* article expressed concern that "there's not a single uncompromising anti-censorship figure or group on the list".[[371]](#cite_note-374)[[372]](#cite_note-375)

Twitter banned 7,000 accounts and limited 150,000 more that had ties to [QAnon](/wiki/QAnon "QAnon") on July 21, 2020. The bans and limits came after QAnon-related accounts began harassing other users through practices of swarming or brigading, coordinated attacks on these individuals through multiple accounts in the weeks prior. Those accounts limited by Twitter will not appear in searches nor be promoted in other Twitter functions. Twitter said they will continue to ban or limit accounts as necessary, with their support account stating "We will permanently suspend accounts Tweeting about these topics that we know are engaged in violations of our multi-account policy, coordinating abuse around individual victims, or are attempting to evade a previous suspension".[[373]](#cite_note-376)

In September 2021, Twitter began beta testing a feature called Safety Mode.[[374]](#cite_note-Peters-2021-377) The functionality aims to limit unwelcome interactions through automated detection of negative engagements. If a user has Safety Mode enabled, authors of tweets that are identified by Twitter's technology as being harmful or exercising uninvited behavior will be temporarily unable to follow the account, send direct messages, or see tweets from the user with the enabled functionality during the temporary block period.[[375]](#cite_note-378) Jarrod Doherty, senior product manager at Twitter, stated that the technology in place within Safety Mode assesses existing relationships to prevent blocking accounts that the user frequently interacts with.[[374]](#cite_note-Peters-2021-377)

### Suspect and contested accounts

See also: [Suspensions on X](/wiki/Suspensions_on_X "Suspensions on X")

In January 2016, Twitter was sued by the widow of a U.S. man killed in the [2015 Amman shooting attack](/wiki/2015_Amman_shooting_attack "2015 Amman shooting attack"), claiming that allowing the [Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant") (ISIL) to continually use the platform, including direct messages in particular,[[376]](#cite_note-verge-revisedisillawsuit-379) constituted the [provision of material support to a terrorist organization](/wiki/Providing_material_support_for_terrorism "Providing material support for terrorism"), which is illegal under U.S. federal law. Twitter disputed the claim, stating that "violent threats and the promotion of terrorism deserve no place on Twitter and, like other social networks, our rules make that clear".[[377]](#cite_note-wsj-isistwitter-380)[[378]](#cite_note-wsj-twitterliable-381) The lawsuit was dismissed by the [United States District Court for the Northern District of California](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_California "United States District Court for the Northern District of California"), upholding the [Section 230](/wiki/Section_230 "Section 230") safe harbor, which dictates that the operators of an interactive computer service are not liable for the content published by its users.[[378]](#cite_note-wsj-twitterliable-381)[[379]](#cite_note-verge-section230isis-382) The lawsuit was revised in August 2016, providing comparisons to other telecommunications devices.[[376]](#cite_note-verge-revisedisillawsuit-379) The second amended complaint was dismissed by the district court, a decision affirmed on appeal to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit on January 31, 2018.[[380]](#cite_note-fields-v-twitter-appeal-383)

Twitter suspended multiple parody accounts that satirized Russian politics in May 2016, sparking protests and raising questions about where the company stands on [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech").[[381]](#cite_note-384) Following public outcry, Twitter restored the accounts the next day without explaining why the accounts had been suspended.[[382]](#cite_note-385) The same day, Twitter, along with Facebook, Google, and [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), jointly agreed to a [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") code of conduct obligating them to review "[the] majority of valid notifications for removal of illegal [hate speech](/wiki/Hate_speech "Hate speech")" posted on their services within 24 hours.[[383]](#cite_note-guardian-euhatespeech-386) In August 2016, Twitter stated that it had banned 235,000 accounts over the past six months, bringing the overall number of suspended accounts to 360,000 accounts in the past year, for violating policies banning use of the platform to promote extremism.[[384]](#cite_note-387) On May 10, 2019, Twitter announced that they suspended 166,513 accounts for promoting terrorism in the July–December 2018 period, saying there was a steady decrease in terrorist groups trying to use the platform owing to its "zero-tolerance policy enforcement". According to [Vijaya Gadde](/wiki/Vijaya_Gadde "Vijaya Gadde"), Legal, Policy and Trust and Safety Lead at Twitter, there was a reduction of 19% terror related tweets from the previous reporting period (January–June 2018).[[385]](#cite_note-388)[[386]](#cite_note-389)[[387]](#cite_note-390)[[388]](#cite_note-391)[[389]](#cite_note-392)

As of July 30, 2020, Twitter will block URLs in tweets that point to external websites that contain malicious content (such as malware and phishing content) as well as hate speech, speech encouraging violence, terrorism, child sexual exploitation, breaches of privacy, and other similar content that is already banned as part of the content of tweets on the site. Users that frequently point to such sites may have their accounts suspended. Twitter said this was to bring their policy in line to prevent users from bypassing their tweet content restrictions by simply linking to the banned content.[[390]](#cite_note-393)

After the onset of protests by [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s supporters across the US in January 2021, Twitter suspended more than 70,000 accounts, stating that they shared "harmful [QAnon](/wiki/QAnon "QAnon")-associated content" at a large scale, and were "dedicated to the propagation of this conspiracy theory across the service".[[391]](#cite_note-394) One of the accounts suspended was then-former-president Trump's account; in February 2025, X settled a lawsuit filed by Trump in response to his suspension paying Trump approximately $10 million.[[392]](#cite_note-395)

### Malicious and fake accounts

Between January and late July 2017, Twitter had identified and shut down over 7,000 fake accounts created by Iranian influence operations.[[393]](#cite_note-396)

In May 2018, in response to scrutiny over the misuse of Twitter by those seeking to maliciously influence elections, Twitter announced that it would partner with the nonprofit organization [Ballotpedia](/wiki/Ballotpedia "Ballotpedia") to add special labels verifying the authenticity of political candidates running for election in the U.S.[[394]](#cite_note-397)[[395]](#cite_note-398) In December 2019, Twitter removed 5,929 accounts for violating their [manipulation](/wiki/Internet_manipulation "Internet manipulation") policies. The company investigated and attributed these accounts to a single state-run information operation, which originated in Saudi Arabia. The accounts were reported to be a part of a larger group of 88,000 accounts engaged in [spammy behavior](/wiki/Spamming "Spamming"). However, Twitter did not disclose all of them as some could possibly be legitimate accounts taken over through [hacking](/wiki/Security_hacker "Security hacker").[[396]](#cite_note-399)

In March 2021, Twitter suspended around 3,500 fake accounts that were running a campaign to influence the American audience, after the US intelligence officials concluded that the assassination of *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* journalist [Jamal Khashoggi](/wiki/Jamal_Khashoggi "Jamal Khashoggi") was "approved" by the Saudi Crown Prince [Mohammed bin Salman](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Salman "Mohammed bin Salman"). These Saudi accounts were working in two languages, English and Arabic, to influence public opinion around the issue. Many accounts commented directly on the tweets of US-based media houses, including *The Post*, *[CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN")*, *[CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News")* and *[The Los Angeles Times](/wiki/The_Los_Angeles_Times "The Los Angeles Times")*. Twitter was unable to identify the source of the influence campaign.[[397]](#cite_note-400)

As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=X_(social_network)&action=edit), the top four countries spreading state-linked Twitter misinformation are Russia, China, Iran and Saudi Arabia.[[398]](#cite_note-aspi2022-401)

In November 2025, X began displaying various information on user accounts for transparency such as location, history, and username changes to combat bots and other malicious accounts.[[399]](#cite_note-402)[[400]](#cite_note-403)[[401]](#cite_note-404) Other X users and media commenters noted seeming inconsistencies between some prominent users' claimed location or nationality and the newly displayed data, with experts claiming that these accounts were likely used for "rage farming" or as foreign influence operations.[[402]](#cite_note-405)[[403]](#cite_note-406)

### Bot accounts

Main article: [Bots on X](/wiki/Bots_on_X "Bots on X")

A bot is a computer program that can automatically tweet, retweet, and follow other accounts. Twitter's open [application programming interface](/wiki/Application_programming_interface "Application programming interface") and the availability of cloud servers make it possible for bots to exist within the social networking site.[[404]](#cite_note-407) Benign bots may generate creative content and relevant product updates, whereas malicious bots can make unpopular people seem popular, push irrelevant products on users, and spread misinformation, spam or slander.[[405]](#cite_note-408) Bots amass significant influence and have been noted to sway elections, influence the stock market, appeal to the public, and attack governments.[[406]](#cite_note-409) As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=X_(social_network)&action=edit), Twitter said there were 20 million fake accounts on Twitter, representing less than 5% of active users.[[407]](#cite_note-410) A 2020 estimate put the figure at 15% of all accounts or around 48 million accounts.[[12]](#cite_note-Rodriguez-14)

## Society

### Usage

Main article: [Twitter usage](/wiki/Twitter_usage "Twitter usage")

[![Man in his twenties smiling at left, man in his forties using computer at center, large crystal chandelier, several people in audience](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Twitter_Town_Hall-Dorsey_Obama.png/250px-Twitter_Town_Hall-Dorsey_Obama.png)](/wiki/File:Twitter_Town_Hall-Dorsey_Obama.png)

[Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey") (left) said after a [Twitter Town Hall with Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama_on_social_media "Barack Obama on social media") held in July 2011, that Twitter received over 110,000 #Ask[Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") tweets.[[408]](#cite_note-411)

#### Protesters

Twitter had been used for a variety of purposes in many industries and scenarios. For example, it has been used to organize protests, including the [protests over the 2009 Moldovan election](/wiki/April_2009_Moldovan_parliamentary_election_protests "April 2009 Moldovan parliamentary election protests"), the [2009 student protests in Austria](/wiki/2009_student_protests_in_Austria "2009 student protests in Austria"), the 2009 [Gaza–Israel conflict](/wiki/Gaza%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "Gaza–Israel conflict"), the 2009 [Iranian green revolution](/wiki/Iranian_Green_Movement "Iranian Green Movement"), the 2010 [Toronto G20 protests](/wiki/2010_G20_Toronto_summit_protests "2010 G20 Toronto summit protests"), the 2010 [Bolivarian Revolution](/wiki/Bolivarian_Revolution "Bolivarian Revolution"), the 2010 [Stuttgart 21](/wiki/Stuttgart_21 "Stuttgart 21") protests in Germany, the 2011 [Egyptian Revolution](/wiki/Egyptian_revolution_of_2011 "Egyptian revolution of 2011"), [2011 England riots](/wiki/2011_England_riots "2011 England riots"), the 2011 United States [Occupy movement](/wiki/Occupy_movement "Occupy movement"), the 2011 [anti-austerity movement in Spain](/wiki/Anti-austerity_movement_in_Spain "Anti-austerity movement in Spain"), the 2011 [Aganaktismenoi movements in Greece](/wiki/Anti-austerity_movement_in_Greece "Anti-austerity movement in Greece"), the 2011 [demonstration in Rome](/wiki/2011_Rome_demonstration "2011 Rome demonstration"), the 2011 [Wisconsin labor protests](/wiki/2011_Wisconsin_protests "2011 Wisconsin protests"), the 2012 [Gaza–Israel conflict](/wiki/Gaza%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "Gaza–Israel conflict"), the [2013 protests in Brazil](/wiki/2013_protests_in_Brazil "2013 protests in Brazil"), and the 2013 [Gezi Park protests](/wiki/Gezi_Park_protests "Gezi Park protests") in Turkey.[[409]](#cite_note-Buettner2016a-412)

The service was also used as a form of [civil disobedience](/wiki/Civil_disobedience "Civil disobedience"): In 2010, users expressed outrage over the [Twitter joke trial](/wiki/Twitter_joke_trial "Twitter joke trial") by copying a controversial joke about bombing an airport and attaching the hashtag #IAmSpartacus, a reference to the film *[Spartacus](/wiki/Spartacus_(film) "Spartacus (film)")* (1960) and a sign of solidarity and support to a man controversially prosecuted after posting a tweet joking about bombing [an airport](/wiki/Doncaster_Sheffield_Airport "Doncaster Sheffield Airport") if they canceled his flight. #IAmSpartacus became the number one trending topic on Twitter worldwide.[[410]](#cite_note-413) Another case of civil disobedience happened in the [2011 British privacy injunction debate](/wiki/2011_British_privacy_injunctions_controversy "2011 British privacy injunctions controversy"), where several celebrities who had taken out anonymized injunctions were identified by thousands of users in protest to traditional journalism being censored.[[411]](#cite_note-414)

#### Governments

According to documents leaked by [Edward Snowden](/wiki/Edward_Snowden "Edward Snowden") and published in July 2014, the United Kingdom's [GCHQ](/wiki/GCHQ "GCHQ") has a tool named BIRDSONG for "automated posting of Twitter updates" and a tool named BIRDSTRIKE for "Twitter monitoring and profile collection".[[412]](#cite_note-415)[[413]](#cite_note-416)

During the [2019–20 Hong Kong protests](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–20 Hong Kong protests"), [Twitter suspended a core group](/wiki/Reactions_to_the_2019%E2%80%9320_Hong_Kong_protests#Other_reactions "Reactions to the 2019–20 Hong Kong protests") of 1,000 "fake" accounts and an associated network of 200,000 accounts for operating a [disinformation](/wiki/Disinformation "Disinformation") campaign that was linked to the [Chinese government](/wiki/Chinese_government "Chinese government").[[414]](#cite_note-417)[[415]](#cite_note-418)[[416]](#cite_note-419)[[417]](#cite_note-420)[[418]](#cite_note-421)

On June 12, 2020, Twitter suspended over 7,000 accounts from Turkey because those accounts were fake profiles, designed to support the Turkish president, [Recep Tayyip Erdoğan](/wiki/Recep_Tayyip_Erdo%C4%9Fan "Recep Tayyip Erdoğan"), and were managed by a central authority. Turkey's communication director said that the decision was illogical, biased, and politically motivated.[[419]](#cite_note-422) Turkey blocked access to Twitter twice, once after voice recordings appeared on Twitter in which Erdoğan ordered his son to stash away millions of dollars and another time for 12 hours in the aftermath of the [earthquake of February 2023](/wiki/2023_Turkey%E2%80%93Syria_earthquake "2023 Turkey–Syria earthquake"), when Erdoğan blamed the people for a disinformation campaign as they criticized the Government for their lack of help.[[420]](#cite_note-423) In May 2021, Twitter labeled one of the tweets by [Sambit Patra](/wiki/Sambit_Patra "Sambit Patra"), a spokesman of the local ruling party [BJP](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") in India, as "manipulated media", leading to Twitter's offices in Delhi and [Gurgaon](/wiki/Gurgaon "Gurgaon") being raided by the local police.[[421]](#cite_note-DelhiRaid-424) Later, the [Indian government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") released a statement in July 2021 claiming Twitter has lost its liability protection concerning user-generated content. This was brought on by Twitter's failure to comply with the [new IT rules](/wiki/Information_Technology_Rules,_2021 "Information Technology Rules, 2021") introduced in 2021, with a filing stating that the company failed to appoint executives to govern user content on the platform.[[422]](#cite_note-425) In 2025, X sued the Indian government for using the IT Act to block tweets and other content on its platform.[[423]](#cite_note-426)

According to a report by [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters"), the United States ran a [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda_in_the_United_States "Propaganda in the United States") campaign to spread disinformation about the [Sinovac](/wiki/Sinovac_Biotech "Sinovac Biotech") Chinese COVID-19 vaccine, including using fake social media accounts on Twitter to spread the disinformation that the Sinovac vaccine contained pork-derived ingredients and was therefore *[haram](/wiki/Haram "Haram")* under [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia").[[424]](#cite_note-:6-427) The campaign primarily targeted people in the Philippines and used a social media hashtag for "China is the virus" in [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language").[[424]](#cite_note-:6-427)

#### Pornographic content

Twitter allows pornographic content as long as it is marked "sensitive" by uploaders, which puts it behind an interstice and hides it from minors.[[425]](#cite_note-428) The "super-follow" feature is said to enable competition with the subscription site [OnlyFans](/wiki/OnlyFans "OnlyFans"), used mainly by sex workers.[[426]](#cite_note-429) Many performers use Twitter's service to market and grow their porn businesses, attracting users to paywalled services like OnlyFans by distributing photos and short video clips as advertisements.[[427]](#cite_note-430)[[428]](#cite_note-431)

In April 2022, Twitter convened a "Red Team" for the project of ACM, "Adult Content Monetization", as it is known internally. Eventually, the project was abandoned, because of the difficulty of implementing Real ID.[[429]](#cite_note-Newton-2022-432)

#### Child sexual exploitation (pre-acquisition by Elon Musk)

A February 2021 report from the company's Health team begins, "While the amount of CSE (child sexual exploitation) online has grown exponentially, Twitter's investment in technologies to detect and manage the growth has not."[[429]](#cite_note-Newton-2022-432)

Until February 2022, the only way for users to flag illegal content was to flag it as "sensitive media", a broad category that left much of the worst material unprioritized for moderation. In a February report, employees wrote that Twitter, along with other Tech Companies have "accelerated the pace of CSE content creation and distribution to a breaking point where manual detection, review, and investigations no longer scale" by allowing pornography and failing to invest in systems that could effectively monitor it. The working group made several recommendations, but they were not taken up and the group was disbanded.[[429]](#cite_note-Newton-2022-432) As part of its efforts to monetize porn, Twitter held an internal investigation which reported in April 2022, "Twitter cannot accurately detect child sexual exploitation and non-consensual nudity at scale."[[429]](#cite_note-Newton-2022-432)

*John Doe et al. v. Twitter*, a civil lawsuit filed in the 9th Circuit Court, alleges that Twitter benefited from sex trafficking and refused to remove the illegal tweets when first informed of them.[[430]](#cite_note-433)[[431]](#cite_note-434) In an [amicus brief](/wiki/Amicus_brief "Amicus brief") filed in the case, the [NCMEC](/wiki/National_Center_for_Missing_%26_Exploited_Children "National Center for Missing & Exploited Children") said, "The children informed the company that they were minors, that they had been 'baited, harassed, and threatened' into making the videos, that they were victims of 'sex abuse' under investigation by law enforcement" but Twitter failed to remove the videos, "allowing them to be viewed by hundreds of thousands of the platform's users".[[429]](#cite_note-Newton-2022-432)

Some major brands, including [Dyson](/wiki/Dyson_(company) "Dyson (company)"), [Mazda](/wiki/Mazda "Mazda"), [Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes"), and [PBS Kids](/wiki/PBS_Kids "PBS Kids") suspended their marketing campaigns and pulled their ads from the platform after an investigation showed that Twitter failed to suspend 70% of the accounts that shared or solicited the prohibited content.[[432]](#cite_note-435)

#### Deepfake pornography

Main article: [Grok sexual deepfake scandal](/wiki/Grok_sexual_deepfake_scandal "Grok sexual deepfake scandal")

In December 2025, social media users reported that X's integrated [chatbot](/wiki/Chatbot "Chatbot"), [Grok](/wiki/Grok_(AI) "Grok (AI)"), would allow users to noncensually [strip](/wiki/Nudity "Nudity") individuals, including [minors](/wiki/Minor_(law) "Minor (law)"), or show them performing sexually explicit and [pornographic](/wiki/Pornography "Pornography") acts. The majority of these prompts were targeted at women and girls.[[433]](#cite_note-436) Images generated by Grok since December 2025 have been disproportionately of people in bikinis, transparent clothes, and the like, with users being able to generate such images through prompts such as "put her in a bikini". This scandal would lead to significant criticism from lawmakers across the world, calls for bans on X, as well as legal crackdowns on X and xAI for, amongst other reasons, the facilitation of sexual abuse, [revenge porn](/wiki/Revenge_porn "Revenge porn"), and [child pornography](/wiki/Child_pornography "Child pornography").

### Impact

#### Emergency use

A practical use for Twitter's real-time functionality is as an effective *de facto* emergency communication system for breaking news. It was neither intended nor designed for high-performance communication, but the idea that it could be used for emergency communication was not lost on the creators, who knew that the service could have wide-reaching effects early on when the company used it to communicate during earthquakes.[[434]](#cite_note-437)
Another practical use that is being studied is Twitter's ability to track epidemics and how they spread.[[435]](#cite_note-438) Additionally Twitter serves as a real-time sensor for natural disasters such as bushfires and earthquakes.[[436]](#cite_note-439)[[437]](#cite_note-440)

#### Education

Twitter has been adopted as a communication and learning tool in educational and research[[438]](#cite_note-441) settings mostly in colleges and universities.[[439]](#cite_note-442)[[440]](#cite_note-443) It has been used as a [backchannel](/wiki/Backchannel "Backchannel") to promote student interactions, especially in large-lecture courses.[[441]](#cite_note-444) Research has found that using Twitter in college courses helps students communicate with each other and faculty, promotes informal learning, allows shy students a forum for increased participation, increases student engagement, and improves overall course grades.[[442]](#cite_note-445)[[443]](#cite_note-446)[[444]](#cite_note-447)

Twitter has been an increasingly growing in the field of education as an effective tool that can be used to encourage learning and idea, or knowledge sharing, in and outside the classroom.[[445]](#cite_note-Carrie-2015-448) By using or creating hashtags, students and educators are able to communicate under specific categories of their choice to enhance and promote education. A broad example of a hashtag used in education is "edchat", to communicate with other teachers and people using that hashtag. Once teachers find someone they want to talk to, they can either direct message the person or narrow down the hashtag to make the topic of the conversation more specific, using hashtags for scichat (science), engchat (English), sschat (social studies).[[445]](#cite_note-Carrie-2015-448)

#### Public figures

[Jonathan Zittrain](/wiki/Jonathan_Zittrain "Jonathan Zittrain"), professor of Internet law at [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School"), said that "the qualities that make Twitter seem inane and half-baked are what makes it so powerful."[[446]](#cite_note-449) In that same vein, and with Sigmund Freud in mind, political communications expert Matthew Auer observed that well-crafted tweets by public figures often deliberately mix trivial and serious information so as to appeal to all three parts of the reader's personality: the id, ego, and superego.[[447]](#cite_note-450) The poets [Mira Gonzalez](/wiki/Mira_Gonzalez "Mira Gonzalez") and [Tao Lin](/wiki/Tao_Lin "Tao Lin") published a book titled *Selected Tweets* featuring selections of their tweets over some eight years.[[448]](#cite_note-451) The novelist [Rick Moody](/wiki/Rick_Moody "Rick Moody") wrote a short story for Electric Literature called "Some Contemporary Characters", composed entirely of tweets.[[449]](#cite_note-452)

Many commentators have suggested that Twitter radically changed the format of reporting due to instant, short, and frequent communication.[[450]](#cite_note-453)[[451]](#cite_note-454) According to *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")* writers Benjamin M. Reilly and Robinson Meyer, Twitter has an outsized impact on the public discourse and media. "Something happens on Twitter; celebrities, politicians and journalists talk about it, and it's circulated to a wider audience by Twitter's algorithms; journalists write about the dustup." This can lead to an argument on a Twitter feed looking like a "debate roiling the country... regular people are left with a confused, agitated view of our current political discourse".[[452]](#cite_note-455) In a 2018 article in the *[Columbia Journalism Review](/wiki/Columbia_Journalism_Review "Columbia Journalism Review")*, Matthew Ingram argued much the same about Twitter's "oversized role" and that it promotes immediacy over newsworthiness.[[453]](#cite_note-Ingram-2021-456) In some cases, inauthentic and provocative tweets were taken up as common opinion in mainstream articles. Writers in several outlets unintentionally cited the opinions of Russian [Internet Research Agency](/wiki/Internet_Research_Agency "Internet Research Agency")-affiliated accounts.[[453]](#cite_note-Ingram-2021-456)[[454]](#cite_note-Luk-457)

#### World leaders

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Donald_Trump_Facebook_Twitter_post_19679418_10159419638630725_2833632475130915488.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_Facebook_Twitter_post_19679418_10159419638630725_2833632475130915488.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_Facebook_Twitter_post_19679418_10159419638630725_2833632475130915488.jpg)

Donald Trump's Twitter post from July 2017

World leaders and their diplomats have taken note of Twitter's rapid expansion and have been increasingly using [Twitter diplomacy](/wiki/Twitter_diplomacy "Twitter diplomacy"), the use of Twitter to engage with foreign publics and their own citizens. US Ambassador to Russia, [Michael A. McFaul](/wiki/Michael_A._McFaul "Michael A. McFaul") has been attributed as a pioneer of international Twitter diplomacy. He used Twitter after becoming ambassador in 2011, posting in English and Russian.[[455]](#cite_note-458) On October 24, 2014, [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") sent her first tweet to mark the opening of the [London Science Museum](/wiki/London_Science_Museum "London Science Museum")'s Information Age exhibition.[[456]](#cite_note-459) A 2013 study by website Twiplomacy found that 153 of the 193 countries represented at the United Nations had established government Twitter accounts.[[457]](#cite_note-twiplomacy-460) The same study also found that those accounts amounted to 505 Twitter handles used by world leaders and their foreign ministers, with their tweets able to reach a combined audience of over 106 million followers.[[457]](#cite_note-twiplomacy-460)

According to an analysis of accounts, the heads of state of 125 countries and 139 other leading politicians have Twitter accounts that have between them sent more than 350,000 tweets and have almost 52 million followers. However, only 30 of these do their own tweeting, more than 80 do not subscribe to other politicians and many do not follow any accounts.[[458]](#cite_note-461)

The Twitter account for the pope was set up in 2012. As of February 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=X_(social_network)&action=edit), it has 18 million followers (@Pontifex).[[459]](#cite_note-462)

### Censorship and moderation

Main article: [Censorship of X](/wiki/Censorship_of_X "Censorship of X")

X is banned completely in Russia,[[460]](#cite_note-463) Iran, China and North Korea,[[461]](#cite_note-464) and has been intermittently blocked in numerous countries, including Egypt, Iraq, Nigeria, Turkey, Venezuela and Turkmenistan, on different basis.[[462]](#cite_note-465)[[463]](#cite_note-466)[[464]](#cite_note-guardian-4jun2021-467)[[465]](#cite_note-468)[[466]](#cite_note-469)[[467]](#cite_note-470) In 2016, Twitter cooperated with the Israeli government to remove certain content originating outside Israel from tweets seen in Israel.[[468]](#cite_note-471) In the 11th biannual transparency report published on September 19, 2017, Twitter said that Turkey was the first among countries where about 90% of removal requests came from, followed by Russia, France and Germany.[[469]](#cite_note-472) Twitter stated that between July 1 and December 31, 2018, "We received legal demands relating to 27,283 accounts from 47 different countries, including Bulgaria, Kyrgyzstan, Macedonia, and Slovenia for the first time."[[470]](#cite_note-473) As part of evidence to a U.S. Senate Enquiry, the company admitted that their systems "detected and hid" several hundred thousand tweets relating to the [2016 Democratic National Committee email leak](/wiki/2016_Democratic_National_Committee_email_leak "2016 Democratic National Committee email leak").[[471]](#cite_note-474) During the curfew in [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)") after [revocation of its autonomous status](/wiki/Indian_revocation_of_Jammu_and_Kashmir%27s_special_status "Indian revocation of Jammu and Kashmir's special status") on August 5, 2019, the Indian government approached Twitter to block accounts accused of spreading anti-India content;[[472]](#cite_note-475) by October 25, nearly one million tweets had been removed as a result.[[473]](#cite_note-476)

In March 2022, shortly after Russia's censorship of Twitter, a Tor [onion](/wiki/.onion ".onion") service link was created by the platform to allow people to access the website, even in countries with heavy Internet censorship.[[474]](#cite_note-477)[[475]](#cite_note-478) In 2025, India ordered X to block 8,000 accounts to users within India, under threat of fines. X criticized the government's orders and encouraged affected users to seek legal recourse.[[476]](#cite_note-479) X uses Age Verify with ID or Photo Selfie for users to access sensitive content like pornography in the UK, EU and EEA to comply with [Online Safety Act 2023](/wiki/Online_Safety_Act_2023 "Online Safety Act 2023") and [EU's Digital Service](/wiki/Digital_Services_Act "Digital Services Act").[[477]](#cite_note-480)

#### Moderation of tweets

Main article: [Censorship by X](/wiki/Censorship_by_X "Censorship by X")

See also: [Twitter suspensions](/wiki/Twitter_suspensions "Twitter suspensions") and [December 2022 Twitter suspensions](/wiki/December_2022_Twitter_suspensions "December 2022 Twitter suspensions")

Twitter removed more than 88,000 propaganda accounts linked to Saudi Arabia.[[478]](#cite_note-481) Twitter removed tweets from accounts associated with the Russian [Internet Research Agency](/wiki/Internet_Research_Agency "Internet Research Agency") that had tried to influence public opinion during and after the 2016 US election.[[453]](#cite_note-Ingram-2021-456)[[454]](#cite_note-Luk-457) In June 2020, Twitter also removed 175,000 propaganda accounts that were spreading biased political narratives for the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"), the [United Russia](/wiki/United_Russia "United Russia") Party, or Turkey's President [Erdogan](/wiki/Recep_Tayyip_Erdo%C4%9Fan "Recep Tayyip Erdoğan"), identified based on centralized behavior.[[479]](#cite_note-482)[[480]](#cite_note-483) Twitter also removed accounts linked to the governments of Armenia, Egypt, Cuba, Serbia, Honduras, Indonesia and Iran.[[481]](#cite_note-484)[[482]](#cite_note-485)[[483]](#cite_note-486) Twitter suspended Pakistani accounts tied to government officials for posting tweets about the [Kashmir conflict](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") between India and Pakistan.[[484]](#cite_note-487) In February 2021, Twitter removed accounts in India that criticized Prime Minister [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi")'s government for its conduct during [Indian farmers' protests in 2020–2021](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932021_Indian_farmers%27_protest "2020–2021 Indian farmers' protest").[[485]](#cite_note-488)

At the start of the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic, numerous tweets reported false medical information related to the pandemic. Twitter announced a new policy in which they would label tweets containing misinformation going forward.[[65]](#cite_note-Roth-2020-68) In April 2020, Twitter removed accounts which defended President [Rodrigo Duterte](/wiki/Rodrigo_Duterte "Rodrigo Duterte")'s response to the spread of COVID-19 in the Philippines.[[486]](#cite_note-489) In November 2020, then Chief Technology Officer and future CEO of Twitter [Parag Agrawal](/wiki/Parag_Agrawal "Parag Agrawal"), when asked by [MIT Technology Review](/wiki/MIT_Technology_Review "MIT Technology Review") about balancing the protection of [free speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") as a core value and the endeavour to combat misinformation, said: "Our role is not to be bound by the [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution"), but our role is to serve a healthy public conversation ... focus less on thinking about free speech, but thinking about how the times have changed."[[487]](#cite_note-490)

Musk had been critical of Twitter's moderation of misinformation prior to his acquisition of the company.[[488]](#cite_note-491) After the transition, Musk eliminated the misinformation moderation team,[[489]](#cite_note-492) and stopped enforcing its policy on labeling tweets with misleading information about coronavirus.[[490]](#cite_note-493) While Twitter had joined a voluntary program under the European Union's to fight disinformation in June 2022, Musk pulled the company out of the program in May 2023.[[491]](#cite_note-494)

### Community Notes

Main article: [Community Notes](/wiki/Community_Notes "Community Notes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Community_Notes_logo.svg/250px-Community_Notes_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Community_Notes_logo.svg)

The logo of Community Notes, November 2022

In August 2020, development of Birdwatch was announced, initially described as a moderation tool. Twitter first launched the Birdwatch program in January 2021, intended as a way to debunk misinformation and propaganda, with a [pilot program](/wiki/Pilot_program "Pilot program") of 1,000 contributors,[[492]](#cite_note-Hollister-2020-495)[[493]](#cite_note-Lyons-2021-496) weeks after the [January 6 United States Capitol attack](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack").[[494]](#cite_note-Elliott-2023-497) The aim was to "build Birdwatch in the open, and have it shaped by the Twitter community". In November 2021, Twitter updated the Birdwatch moderation tool to limit the visibility of contributors' identities by creating aliases for their accounts, in an attempt to limit bias towards the author of notes.[[493]](#cite_note-Lyons-2021-496)[[495]](#cite_note-498)

Twitter then expanded access to notes made by the Birdwatch contributors in March 2022, giving a randomized set of US users the ability to view notes attached to tweets and rate them,[[496]](#cite_note-499) with a pilot of 10,000 contributors.[[497]](#cite_note-500) On average, contributors were noting 43 times a day in 2022 prior to the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"). This then increased to 156 on the day of the invasion, estimated to be a very small portion of the misleading posts on the platform. By March 1, only 359 of 10,000 contributors had proposed notes in 2022, while a Twitter spokeswoman described plans to scale up the program, with the focus on "ensuring that Birdwatch is something people find helpful and can help inform understanding".[[498]](#cite_note-501)[[499]](#cite_note-502)

By September 2022, the program had expanded to 15,000 users.[[500]](#cite_note-503) In October 2022, the most commonly published notes were related to [COVID-19 misinformation](/wiki/COVID-19_misinformation "COVID-19 misinformation") based on historical usage.[[501]](#cite_note-Faife-2022-504) In November 2022, at the request of [new owner Elon Musk](/wiki/Twitter_under_Elon_Musk "Twitter under Elon Musk"), Birdwatch was rebranded to [Community Notes](/wiki/Community_Notes "Community Notes"), taking an [open-source](/wiki/Open_source "Open source") approach to deal with misinformation,[[502]](#cite_note-Sankaran-2022-505) and expanded to Europe and countries outside of the US.[[503]](#cite_note-Biron-2022-506)[[504]](#cite_note-Baldacchino-2023-507)[[505]](#cite_note-Leloup-2023-508)

### Court cases, lawsuits, and adjudication

*[Twitter Inc. v. Taamneh](/wiki/Twitter,_Inc._v._Taamneh "Twitter, Inc. v. Taamneh")*, alongside *[Gonzalez v. Google](/wiki/Gonzalez_v._Google_LLC "Gonzalez v. Google LLC")*, were heard by the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") during its 2022–2023 term. Both cases dealt with Internet content providers and whether they are liable for terrorism-related information posted by their users. In the case of *Twitter v. Taamneh*, the case asked if Twitter and other social media services are liable for [user-generated](/wiki/User-generated_content "User-generated content") terrorism content under the [Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996](/wiki/Antiterrorism_and_Effective_Death_Penalty_Act_of_1996 "Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996") and are beyond their [Section 230](/wiki/Section_230 "Section 230") protections. The court ruled in May 2023 that the charges brought against Twitter and other companies were not permissible under the Antiterrorism Act, and did not address the Section 230 question. This decision also supported the Court's [*per curiam* decision](/wiki/Per_curiam "Per curiam") in *Gonzalez* returning that case to the lower court for review in light of the *Twitter* decision.[[506]](#cite_note-nbcnews_decision-509)[[507]](#cite_note-510)

In 2016, Twitter shareholder Doris Shenwick filed a lawsuit against Twitter, Inc., claiming executives misled investors over the company's growth prospects.[[508]](#cite_note-Time-511) In 2021, Twitter agreed to pay $809.5 million to settle.[[508]](#cite_note-Time-511)

In May 2022, Twitter agreed to pay $150 million to settle a lawsuit started by the Department of Justice and the Federal Trade Commission. The lawsuit concerned Twitter's use of email addresses and phone numbers of Twitter users to [target advertisements](/wiki/Targeted_advertising "Targeted advertising") at them. The company also agreed to third-party audits of its [data privacy](/wiki/Information_privacy "Information privacy") program.[[509]](#cite_note-512) On November 3, 2022, on the eve of expected layoffs, a group of Twitter employees based in San Francisco and Cambridge filed a lawsuit in the U.S. District Court in San Francisco. Naming five current or former workers as plaintiffs, the suit accused the company of violating federal and state laws that govern notice of employment termination.[[510]](#cite_note-513) The federal law in question is the [Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) Act](/wiki/Worker_Adjustment_and_Retraining_Notification_Act_of_1988 "Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act of 1988"), and the state law in question is California's state WARN Act.[[511]](#cite_note-514)

On November 20, 2023, X filed a lawsuit against [Media Matters](/wiki/Media_Matters_for_America "Media Matters for America"), a media watchdog group. The lawsuit alleges defamation by Media Matters following its publication of a report claiming that advertisements for major brands were displayed alongside posts promoting [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") and the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party").[[512]](#cite_note-515)

On August 6, 2024, X filed an [antitrust](/wiki/Competition_law "Competition law") lawsuit in the [Northern District of Texas](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_Texas "United States District Court for the Northern District of Texas") against the [World Federation of Advertisers](/wiki/World_Federation_of_Advertisers "World Federation of Advertisers"), [Unilever](/wiki/Unilever "Unilever"), [Mars](/wiki/Mars_Inc. "Mars Inc."), [CVS](/wiki/CVS_Health "CVS Health") and [Ørsted](/wiki/%C3%98rsted_(company) "Ørsted (company)"), alleging that the advertisers had conspired via their participation in the Global Alliance for Responsible Media to withhold "billions of dollars in advertising revenue" from the platform.[[513]](#cite_note-:0-516) The World Federation Of Advertisers created the Global Alliance for Responsible Media in 2019 to address "illegal or harmful content on digital media platforms and its monetization via advertising".[[514]](#cite_note-517) On August 13, 2024, the [Workplace Relations Commission](/wiki/Workplace_Relations_Commission "Workplace Relations Commission") ordered X to pay €550,000 to former senior staffer Gary Rooney in an unfair dismissal case. X had argued that Rooney's failure to check "yes" at the bottom of an email from Elon Musk constituted resignation.[[515]](#cite_note-518)[[516]](#cite_note-519)

In August 2024, the Federal Supreme Court of [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") blocked X throughout the country due to their alleged non-compliance to Brazilian judicial rulings. The platform resumed its operations in the country in October 2024, after complying with judicial requests.[[517]](#cite_note-520)

## Criticism

Main article: [Criticism of X (social network)](/wiki/Criticism_of_X_(social_network) "Criticism of X (social network)")

Twitter has faced significant controversy since its acquisition Musk and rebranding to X, including an increase in misinformation, hate speech and antisemitism.[[16]](#cite_note-Hickey-2023-18)[[518]](#cite_note-521)[[519]](#cite_note-522) According to a report published by the ["Never Again" Association](/wiki/%22Never_Again%22_Association "\"Never Again\" Association"), X refuses to remove hate speech or ignores reports.[[520]](#cite_note-523)[[521]](#cite_note-524)

Researchers have called for greater transparency especially ahead of national elections, based on findings that the platform algorithm favors a small number of popular accounts, in particular right-leaning users.[[522]](#cite_note-525)

In July 2025, Musk and xAI's artificial intelligence tool, Grok, faced backlash from X users and the [Anti-Defamation League](/wiki/Anti-Defamation_League "Anti-Defamation League") regarding a series of antisemitic tweets made in response to the [July 2025 Central Texas floods](/wiki/July_2025_Central_Texas_floods "July 2025 Central Texas floods").[[523]](#cite_note-526) The Grok account acknowledged the "inappropriate" posts and removed the comments. The incident is reported to have happened just days after Musk announced updates to Grok, noting that users should see "a difference when you ask Grok questions."[[524]](#cite_note-527)

### Record tweets

Main articles: [List of most-retweeted tweets](/wiki/List_of_most-retweeted_tweets "List of most-retweeted tweets") and [List of most-liked tweets](/wiki/List_of_most-liked_tweets "List of most-liked tweets")

The "[Oscar Selfie](/wiki/Oscar_Selfie "Oscar Selfie")" orchestrated by [86th Academy Awards](/wiki/86th_Academy_Awards "86th Academy Awards") host [Ellen DeGeneres](/wiki/Ellen_DeGeneres "Ellen DeGeneres") during the March 2, 2014, broadcast was, at the time, the most retweeted image ever.[[525]](#cite_note-ellen_2014-529) The photo of twelve celebrities broke the previous retweet record within forty minutes and was retweeted over 1.8 million times in the first hour.[[526]](#cite_note-bbcrt-530)[[527]](#cite_note-531)[[528]](#cite_note-latos-532) On May 9, 2017, Ellen's record was broken by Carter Wilkerson (@carterjwm) by collecting nearly 3.5 million retweets in a little over a month.[[529]](#cite_note-533) This record was broken when [Yusaku Maezawa](/wiki/Yusaku_Maezawa "Yusaku Maezawa") announced a giveaway on Twitter in January 2019, accumulating 4.4 million retweets. A similar tweet he made in December 2019 was retweeted 3.8 million times.[[530]](#cite_note-534)

The most tweeted moment in the history of Twitter occurred on August 2, 2013; during a Japanese television airing of the [Studio Ghibli](/wiki/Studio_Ghibli "Studio Ghibli") film *[Castle in the Sky](/wiki/Castle_in_the_Sky "Castle in the Sky")*, fans simultaneously tweeted the word *balse* (バルス)—the incantation for a destruction spell used during its climax, after it was uttered in the film. There was a global peak of 143,199 tweets in one second, beating the previous record of 33,388.[[531]](#cite_note-535)[[532]](#cite_note-536) The most discussed event in Twitter history occurred on October 24, 2015; the hashtag ("#ALDubEBTamangPanahon") for *[Tamang Panahon](/wiki/Tamang_Panahon "Tamang Panahon")*, a live special episode of the Filipino variety show *[Eat Bulaga!](/wiki/Eat_Bulaga! "Eat Bulaga!")* at the [Philippine Arena](/wiki/Philippine_Arena "Philippine Arena"), centering on its popular on-air couple [AlDub](/wiki/AlDub "AlDub"), attracted 41 million tweets.[[533]](#cite_note-537)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*][[534]](#cite_note-538) The most-discussed sporting event in Twitter history was the [2014 FIFA World Cup semi-final](/wiki/Brazil_v_Germany_(2014_FIFA_World_Cup) "Brazil v Germany (2014 FIFA World Cup)") between Brazil and Germany on July 8, 2014.[[535]](#cite_note-539)

According to *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")*, the fastest pace to a million followers was set by actor [Robert Downey Jr.](/wiki/Robert_Downey_Jr. "Robert Downey Jr.") in 23 hours and 22 minutes in April 2014.[[536]](#cite_note-540) This record was later broken by [Caitlyn Jenner](/wiki/Caitlyn_Jenner "Caitlyn Jenner"), who joined the site on June 1, 2015, and amassed a million followers in just 4 hours and 3 minutes.[[537]](#cite_note-541)
